segments.s659-g297: Word order is inverted for stylistic/poetic effect: 'فرستاده را گفت شاه' = 'the king said to the messenger.' 'ازان پس' ('after that' / 'thereupon') sets the temporal frame. 'گفت' is the past-tense reporting verb introducing the following speech.
segments.s659-g298: The conjunction 'که' introduces the reported commands that follow. 'پیغام بگزار' and 'پاسخ بخواه' are imperatives formed with the classical prefix 'ب-' (present/imperfective imperative): 'begoẓār' = 'deliver/put forth the message', 'bexāh' = 'ask/demand a reply.' The two verbs are joined by 'و' ('and').
segments.s659-g299: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). 'بشنید' is a third-person past ('he heard'). 'رومی' is the subject ('the Roman'), and 'زبان برگشاد' = literally 'opened (his) tongue' — a poetic way of saying 'began to speak.' 'برگشاد' is a past participle/past form meaning 'opened.'
segments.s659-g300: The noun phrase 'سخن‌های قیصر' ('Caesar's words/speeches') is the object of the verbal phrase 'همه کرد یاد.' In classical Persian 'یاد کردن' = 'to remember'; here 'کرد' is the auxiliary/past 'did/made' and 'یاد' is the noun functioning with it to mean 'remembered' — 'he remembered all of Caesar's words.'
segments.s659-g301: 'که گفت' introduces reported speech ('he said that'). Here 'از' functions as 'concerning' or 'regarding' ('of/regarding the sovereign'), so 'از جهاندار پیروز جنگ' = 'of the world-ruler victorious in war' (a descriptive genitive/agent phrase).
segments.s659-g302: 'خرد باید' is a nominal predicate: 'wisdom is necessary' (باید = 'it is necessary' / 'must'). The following nouns joined by 'و' form a list of required qualities: 'دانش' (learning), 'نام' (fame/reputation), and 'ننگ' (here part of the paired formula).
segments.s659-g303: The construction 'تو را ... هست' expresses possession or attribution to 'you' ('for you there is ...' = 'you have ...'). 'فر و برز جهاندار' is a compound noun phrase: 'فر' = divine/royal glory, 'برز' = exaltedness/height, qualified by 'جهاندار' = 'of the sovereign.' 'هست' is the copula/existential verb.
segments.s659-g304: A parallel list of attributes: 'بزرگی' (greatness), 'دانایی' (wisdom/knowledge), and 'زور دست' ('the force/power of the hand' — an idiom for might). The nouns are simply coordinated by 'و' and complete what was said to belong to 'you.'
segments.s659-g417: فروشید is a plural imperative ('sell' addressed to multiple sellers). و simply links clauses ('and'). افزون here functions as an adverb meaning 'more' / 'additional' and precedes the verb it modifies. مجویید is a prohibitive/negative form meaning 'do not seek/ask' (2nd-person plural negative imperative). نیز adds emphasis/background sense 'also' or 'besides'. Together this is an instruction: 'Sell and do not ask for anything more.'
segments.s659-g418: که introduces a causal clause here ('for/because'). ما is the subject 'we'. بی‌نیازیم is a predicative adjective with the 1st-person-plural ending meaning 'we are without need / self-sufficient' (present). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ایشان is the pronoun 'them'. به‌چیز ('for anything' / 'for any thing') completes the idea: 'we are not in need of anything from them.'
segments.s659-g419: به شمشیر is a prepositional phrase indicating means ('by/with the sword'). خواهیم is the 1st-person-plural form that here expresses future/action ('we will' / 'we shall [take]'); the main verb of the clause is understood with گهر as its object. ز ایشان marks the source ('from them'). The literal order places the instrumental phrase before the verb: 'By sword we will [take] from them (a) jewel.'
segments.s659-g420: همان is a demonstrative pointing back ('that same' / 'those very'). The following nouns are a list of valuables joined by و: بدره and برده (poetic/item names here listed as valuables), and then سیم و زر ('silver and gold'). The sequence names the specific kinds of treasures intended to be taken.
segments.s659-g421: بگفتند is a past narrative verb 'they said' (reported speech). کز is the relative/conjunction 'that'/'who' introducing the content clause. مایه‌داران شهر is a noun phrase 'the wealthy (مایه‌داران) of the city' with شهر as the genitive/possessive adjunct.
segments.s659-g422: دو بازارگانند is a nominal predicate: 'they are two merchants' (بازارگانند = 'are merchants' / 'they trade'). کز is again 'who/that'. شب means 'night' and دو بهر ('two bahrs') here denotes 'on two occasions / twice' (poetic use of بهر = occasion/turn). So the clause says there are two merchants who, twice each night, (do something — continued in the next line).
segments.s659-g423: یکی را literally 'one (of them)' is topicalized with را. نیاید is a negative present-form meaning 'does not come' / 'does not happen' — with سر اندر به‌خواب ('head into sleep') this idiom means 'he cannot fall asleep' or 'sleep does not come to him.' Word order places the topicalized 'one' before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s659-g424: از introduces the cause ('because of' / 'from'). آواز here means 'song' or musical sound, modified by مستان ('of the revelers/drunkards'), so 'the singing of the revelers.' The instruments چنگ (harp) and رباب (rebab, a bowed instrument) are appended with و to list the sounds that prevent sleep.
segments.s659-g337: همه موبدان آفرین خواندند is a straightforward past-tense clause: subject (همه موبدان ‘all the mobeds’) + predicate (آفرین خواندند ‘they uttered praise/acclamation’). خواندند is the simple past plural of خواندن used here in the sense of uttering/performing an acclamation rather than ‘reading’ in the modern sense.
segments.s659-g338: بدان دانشی گوهر افشاندند is another past action; بِدان(=به + آن) دانشی functions as an adverbial phrase ‘with that knowledge/skill’ (poetic contraction بِدان), and گوهر افشاندند ‘they scattered jewels’ is the verb phrase (افشاندند past plural = they scattered/sprinkled). Word order places the instrument/means before the verb, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s659-g339: شهنشاه رخساره بی‌تاب کرد is causative-stative in past tense: subject شهنشاه (the king) + object/locution رخساره بی‌تاب (his visage restless) + کرد (made/did). Here کرد functions as ‘caused to be,’ so the phrase means the king’s face became/agitated or he made his expression restless.
segments.s659-g340: دهانش پر از در خوشاب کرد uses the pattern ‘X را/ـش پر از Y کردن’ = ‘to fill X with Y.’ دهانش (his mouth) is the affected part, پر از … کرد is the predicate ‘filled with …,’ and در خوشاب is the thing filling it (poetic phrasing for pleasant/sweet water). The verb کرد is past tense causative ‘made/fill(ed).’
segments.s659-g341: ز کار گذشته دلش تنگ شد is a cause-result clause: ز کار گذشته ‘because of what had happened / from the past affair’ (ز = poetic ‘from/because of’; گذشته = ‘the past’), followed by دلش تنگ شد ‘his heart became tight’ (شد = past of شدن, here inchoative ‘became’ — tensing that indicates sorrow/yearning in this idiom).
segments.s659-g342: بپیچید و رویش پر آژنگ شد links two past actions with و: بپیچید ‘he twisted/writhed’ (past), and then رویش پر آژنگ شد ‘his face became full of آژنگ.’ پر = ‘full (of)’, شد = ‘became.’ The exact color/quality آژنگ is a poetic/archaic noun (here indicating a discoloration or strange appearance); grammatically this is a coordinate clause showing a physical reaction following the previous pain.
segments.s659-g343: که با او چرا کرد چندان جفا is a subordinate (rhetorical) clause introduced by که and asking/explaining motive: با او ‘toward him’ + چرا کرد ‘why did (he) do’ (چرا is an interrogative ‘why’ used in narrative questions) + چندان جفا ‘so much cruelty’ as the object. Word order places the indirect object/target (با او) before the verb, and the quantity/emphasis (چندان) modifies the noun جفا.
segments.s659-g344: ازان پس کزو دید مهر و وفا is a temporal/consequential question: از آن پس ‘after that’ sets the time, کزو ‘from whom/from which’ functions as the interrogative agent, and دید مهر و وفا ‘would see love and fidelity’ uses دید (past of دیدن) in a rhetorical sense ‘would one see/expect’ — together forming ‘after that, from whom would one see kindness and faithfulness?’
segments.s659-g97: بدو = به + او, a clitic prepositional phrase meaning 'to him/unto him'. گفت here is the past verb 'said' (classical past) but the provided sense list doesn't include the verbal sense. رو پیش = 'turn/toward before/in the presence of' (رو = 'toward', پیش = 'in the presence of'); دانا is the adjective/noun 'the wise one'. بگوی is the (imperative/subjunctive) form of گفتن 'say' used here as a command or reported imperative ('tell'). The overall clause is an instruction: 'He said to him: go/ tell (the) wise (one)...'.
segments.s659-g98: کزان = 'کِز + ان' = 'from which / from whom' introducing origin. نامور = 'famed/renowned' modifying جاه 'rank/status; honor'. و links the two nouns; آن آبروی = 'that reputation/honor' with آن as a demonstrative determiner. Together: 'from him the famed rank and that reputation.'
segments.s659-g99: چراجستی is the fused question 'چرا جستی' = 'why did you seek' (archaic/poetic past interrogative). از introduces the source/measure 'from/of'; برتری = 'preeminence, superiority'; کمتری = 'lesser' (comparative). The phrase asks 'Why did you seek a lesser superiority?'
segments.s659-g100: ببد is the classical past/copulative 'بَـبُد/بَـشد' meaning 'it was/it became' (here 'became'). گوهر = 'gem/jewel' (predicate noun). و = 'and'. ناسزا = 'insult/abuse' used as a noun; داوری = 'judgment' or 'adjudication' — here a noun in a nominal phrase. Grammatically the verb ببد governs the coordinated predicate 'گوهر و ناسزا داوری' (it became a gem and an insulting judgment).
segments.s659-g101: پرستنده = 'devotee/adorer' (agent noun). بشنید is the past 'he heard'. و = 'and'. آمد (past) 'he came'. دوان is an adverbial/participial form meaning 'running/rushingly' (manner adverb). Together: 'The devotee heard and came running.'
segments.s659-g102: برخال = 'بر + خال' literally 'upon the mole/beauty-spot' (a locative phrase: 'to/at the beauty-spot'). شد is the past/copula 'became/arrived'. تند و خسته = two adjectives coordinated ('swift/rapid' and 'tired/weary'); روان here qualifies state/manner ('in spirit/moving'), so the phrase describes his condition/manner: 'he arrived at the beauty-spot, swift and weary of spirit.' Note the poetic compactness: adjectives follow the copula and modify the subject's state.
segments.s659-g103: ز (poetic) = 'from/of/before' here introducing the locus or relation with شاه 'king'. آنچ = 'that which / those things'. بشیند is an archaic/polite past subjunctive/optative form meaning 'he sat' or 'having sat down' (classical usage). با او بگفت = 'he spoke with him' (با او = 'with him'; بگفت = past 'said/spoke'). The clause presents the sequence: 'Having sat before the king, he spoke with him.'
segments.s659-g104: چنین = 'thus/so' (manner adverb). یافت = past 'found/obtained'. زو = 'از + او' = 'from him'. پاسخ = 'answer/reply' (direct object). اندر = 'in/within'. نهفت = 'hidden/concealed' (adjective/postpositive). Literally: 'Thus he obtained from him an answer hidden within.' Grammatically یافت governs the object phrase 'زو پاسخ اندر نهفت.'
segments.s659-g105: که here is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the clause. حال من is a noun + possessive pronoun construction ('my condition/state') with the possessor after the noun (typical Persian order). از حال شاه جهان is a comparative prepositional phrase: از ('than/of') + noun phrase 'the condition of the king of the world', so the clause literally says 'my state than the state of the king of the world'.
segments.s659-g106: فراوان is an adverb/modifier meaning 'much' or 'by far', modifying the following copula-like verb. بهست is a poetic/copular form meaning 'is' / 'is abundant' (here functioning as a 3rd-person predicative 'is much'); آشکار و نهان are two opposites ('open/manifest and hidden/secret') joined by و to indicate both public and private aspects. The structure is predicative: '[is] much, (both) manifest and hidden.'
segments.s659-g107: پرستنده is the subject 'the devotee/worshipper'. برگشت is the simple past 'returned/turned back'. و links this clause to the next action. پاسخ ببرد is a compact poetic phrasing where ببرد (from برد) means 'took/carried (away)' or 'received' — here functioning as a past action: 'and (he) took the answer.'
segments.s659-g108: سخنها is the plural object 'the words/speeches/statements'. یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / individually'. برو here is an archaic/directional particle (in classical usage it can indicate direction or be a fossilized linker) linking to the verb that follows; in this context it helps mark the manner/sequence of recounting. برشمرد is the past-simple verb 'he enumerated/recounted'.
segments.s659-g109: فراوان is an adverb 'greatly/much'. ز (poetic form of از) marks cause/agent here: 'because of / at' the following noun. پاسخ is the cause noun 'the answers'. برآشفت is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'became angry' or 'was enraged'. شاه (the king) appears after the verb by poetic inversion; he is the one who became angry.
segments.s659-g110: ورا is the enclitic/archaic object meaning 'to him' / 'him' (accusative). بند is a noun 'fetter/ bond' used with فرمود (a light causative) to mean 'he ordered him bound' (literal: 'he commanded [the] binding of him'). فرمود here is a causative/imperative-like narration 'he commanded/ordered'. و ties on تاریک چاه, 'a dark pit' (تاریک modifies چاه); the verb implying 'to put/throw into' is left implicit but understood from the context.
segments.s659-g111: دگر باره is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'again' or 'once more'. پرسید is the past verb 'he asked'. زان is a demonstrative 'that' (poetic form of از + آن or 'that previously mentioned'), and پیشکار is the noun 'attendant/steward' — together: 'he asked that attendant again.'
segments.s659-g112: که begins an indirect question 'how/that' and چُـون here means 'how'. دارد is the present/past narrative verb 'has / does' used to ask about the condition or behaviour ('how does ... fare'). آن کم خرد روزگار is a descriptive noun phrase: آن 'that', کم خرد literally 'little-witted' (i.e. petty-minded or foolish) modifying روزگار 'time / fate / the world' — 'that petty-minded fate/the times'.
segments.s659-g481: دگر (preposed) means “another” and modifies موبدی (the subject, a Zoroastrian priest). گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’ following the subject; کز is a subordinating/relative particle here introducing the reported content (‘that/from which’), followed by شهریار ‘the king.’ The sequence Subject + گفت + کز + complement is a common poetic order for reported speech.
segments.s659-g482: چنین is a fronted predicative adverb ‘thus/so.’ بود is the past copula ‘was.’ پیمان is the nominal predicate ‘a pact/covenant,’ and به یک روزگار is a prepositional phrase marking duration/extent (‘for one time/one age’). Together they state that ‘such was the covenant for a single age.’
segments.s659-g483: که at the start introduces a subordinate clause (‘that/so that’). مردی is an indefinite noun ‘a man’ (the -ی marks indefiniteness). گزینند is 3rd-person plural optative/subjunctive (‘they should choose’ / ‘let them choose’), and فرخ نژاد is a compound noun phrase ‘of fortunate/fortunate-born lineage’ modifying the chosen man.
segments.s659-g484: که here functions as a relative/continuation particle ‘who/that.’ در پادشاهی is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in (the) kingship/kingdom.’ بگردد is a 3rd-person singular present subjunctive/optative of گردیدن used to express the hoped-for action (‘may he go about / govern / be active’), and به‌داد is an adverbial phrase ‘with justice’ (‘justly’), describing how he should rule.
segments.s659-g485: رساند is a past tense verb ‘he delivered / conveyed’ (direct action). بدین is the prepositional phrase ‘to this’ (poetic contraction of به + این) and بارگاه is the indirect object ‘(to) this court/palace.’ آگهی is the direct object ‘notice / information’ placed after the indirect-object phrase for poetic word order.
segments.s659-g486: ز is the preposition ‘about / concerning’ (poetic for از). بسیار و اندک are a paired contrast ‘the many and the few.’ بدی‌گر and بهی form a parallel contrast ‘the evildoer (bad one) and the good,’ so the whole phrase lists both populous/rare cases and wicked/good parties—the content of the report.
segments.s659-g487: گشسب سرافراز is a proper name plus epithet (‘Gushasp the proud/lofty’) serving as the subject. مردی‌ست is the copular nominal phrase ‘is a man’ (ست = poetic form of است), and پیر is an appositive/adjective ‘old,’ giving the characterization ‘Gushasp the proud is an old man.’
segments.s659-g488: سزد‌گر is an agentive noun ‘one who deems it fitting’ or ‘a fitting one.’ بود is the past copula used here to express a potential/optative sense (‘it would be / it was fitting’). داد را دستگیر is a noun phrase with داد ‘justice’ marked by the object/topic marker را and دستگیر used substantively as ‘supporter/assistant’ (literally ‘hand-catcher’), i.e. ‘a helper of justice.’ The whole line says it would be fitting for him to be justice’s helper.
segments.s659-g561: به پیش صف رومیان حمله برد is a compact, poetic word order: به پیش + noun (پیش صف رومیان) gives the direction/target 'toward the front of the Roman line', حمله (attack) is the object/nominal action and برد is the verbal element 'took/carried out' attached after it. In older Persian poetry برد here functions as the final action-word after the noun phrase rather than a separate auxiliary; the whole phrase means 'he made an attack on the Roman ranks'.
segments.s659-g562: بمرد او وزو کودکان ماند خرد compresses several items: بمرد (poetic past/optative form related to 'مرد' — here narratively 'he died'), او 'he', و ز او written together as وزو = 'and from him / and of him', کودکان 'children' (object), ماند 'remained/was left' and خرد 'small' (descriptive). Word order is typical of classical Persian: verb(s) follow their noun-phrases, and adjectival note (خرد) follows the noun it qualifies (کودکان).
segments.s659-g563: چه فرمان دهد شهریار جهان؟ is a rhetorical question. چه 'what', فرمان 'command/order', دهد is the present/subjunctive form of 'دادن' 'to give' used here as 'can/give', and شهریار جهان 'the sovereign (lord) of the world' is the agent. Classical Persian often uses the present/subjunctive/optative to pose what might be done or given.
segments.s659-g564: ز کار چنان خرد کودک نوان؟ literally 'From the deed so great / of such a deed, (how) خرد کودک نوان?' — ز کار 'from the action/affair', چنان 'such', خرد here means 'wisdom' (not 'small' in this line), کودک 'child', and نوان is an archaic/poetic form (here functioning like توان 'is able / can be endowed'). The clause asks rhetorically how a child could acquire such wisdom from that single deed; note the inverted, terse poetic word order.
segments.s659-g565: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past form meaning 'he commanded/said'. کان here functions as a subordinating particle ('that'/'so that' in classical usage) introducing the content of the order, and کودکان‌را is the object in the accusative case-marker form (children as beneficiaries/recipients). Note that Persian poetry can split numeral phrases, so چهار here pairs with هزار in the next segment to make 'four thousand'.
segments.s659-g566: ز گنج درم داد باید هزار is the remainder of the gift-order: ز گنج 'from the treasury/treasure', درم 'dirhams', داد 'gave' (past or causative sense within the reported command), باید 'must/ought'. The numeral هزار ('thousand') is syntactically linked with the previous چهار to mean 'four thousand dirhams must be given'. The verb-like داد follows the noun-phrase as is typical in this poetic register.
segments.s659-g567: هرآنکس که شد کشته در کارزار is a relative clause: هرآنکس 'whoever/anyone who', که شد 'who became / who was' (شد used as past auxiliary), کشته 'slain/killed' (past participle), در کارزار 'in the battle'. Classical Persian places participles and locatives after the subject phrase, forming 'whoever was slain in battle'.
segments.s659-g568: کزو خرد کودک بود یادگار: کزو 'from whom / of whom', خرد here means 'small' (descriptive of the child), کودک 'child', بود 'was' (past copula), یادگار 'a memento/keepsake'. The clause states that from the slain man a small child remained as a memorial — note the order 'source (کزو) + modifier (خرد) + noun (کودک) + copula + predicate (یادگار)'.
segments.s659-g425: چنین داد پاسخ is a classical word order: چنین (thus) + داد (gave) + پاسخ (an answer) = “thus gave an answer” (i.e. “he answered thus”). کزین is a fused poetic preposition + demonstrative (کز + این) meaning “from this / because of this,” and نیست رنج literally negates the noun رنج (“there is no suffering/trouble”). Together this segment reports the content of the speaker’s reply: “thus he replied that from this there is no suffering.”
segments.s659-g426: جز ایشان = “except them / except those”; جز introduces the exception. هرآنکس که دارند گنج is a relative clause: هرآنکس که = “whoever / anyone who,” دارند is the classical present (third plural) meaning “they have / possess,” and گنج is the object “treasure.” Poetic word order places دارند before its object (دارند گنج) and uses ایشان to pick out the excepted people (“those who have treasure”).
segments.s659-g427: همه (everyone) is the subject; همچنان (still / as before) qualifies the state that follows. شاد و خرم are two coordinating adjectives meaning “happy and merry/joyful.” زیند is the classical third‑person plural present/subjunctive form of زیستن used optatively here (often translated “may they live” or “let them live”), so the whole clause expresses a wish: “May everyone remain happy and joyful.”
segments.s659-g428: که here introduces the following explanatory/result clause (can be read as “so that / namely that”). آزاد باشند uses the classical subjunctive/optative باشـَند (“may they be / be they”) with the adjective آزاد (“free”). و links the two predicates; بی‌غم is an adjective “carefree,” and زیند repeats the optative verb “may they live.” In short: “that they be free and live free of grief.”
segments.s659-g429: نوشتند is past plural “they wrote/inscribed.” خطی is an indefinite object “a line / an inscription.” That is followed by که introducing the content of the inscription (reported clause). انوشه is a poetic adjective here meaning “everlasting / long‑lasting” (used adverbially), and بدی is the noun “evil,” beginning the quoted wish: “that evil …” — i.e. they wrote the words expressing a wish against evil.
segments.s659-g430: همیشه (always) sets the temporal scope. ز تو = “from you / away from you.” دور is “far / distant.” دست بدی here is a poetic expression meaning “the hand/power of evil”; note the ezāfe (دستِ بدی) is omitted in this poetic line. Together: “may the hand (power) of evil be always far from you.”
segments.s659-g431: به ایوان is a locative prepositional phrase “to / at the iwan (portico).” چنین گفت is “thus said” (چنین = thus, گفت = said — classical past of گفتن). شاه یمن follows the verb as the subject in the typical classical/Sasanian-influenced word order: ‘the King of Yemen said thus.’
segments.s659-g432: که introduces the subordinate clause (reported content). نوشین‌روان is an epithet “sweet‑souled / pleasant‑minded” functioning as the subject of the following verb. چون has a temporal/conditional sense here (“when / as”), and گشاید is the classical present/subjunctive form of گشودن meaning “opens.” دهن is the object “mouth.” So the clause reads “that when the sweet‑souled one opens his mouth …” (leading on to what follows).
segments.s659-g121: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the material or source; پیکان is the noun 'arrows' (direct object/element); وز here functions as an archaic connective linking the two items (effectively 'and' in this construction, an older variant of و), میخ is 'nails' (iron pins); گرد + اندرش is an adverbial phrase 'around him' (گرد = 'around/about', اندرِ + ش = 'in/at him/it' with the enclitic ش = 'his/its'). Word order is poetic: list of things + locative 'around him'.
segments.s659-g122: هم 'also/too' modifies the following clause; از here means 'by/from' (marking the agent/means), بند آهن is a compound 'iron band/fetter' (بند = fetter/band, آهن = iron), نهفته is the adjectival/predicative participle 'hidden/covered', and سرش 'his head' is the possessed noun with enclitic ش. The phrase is elliptical/poetic: literally 'also from an iron band hidden his head' → 'his head was also hidden by an iron band.'
segments.s659-g123: بدو is the dative/enclitic pronoun phrase 'to him/for him'; اندرون 'inside/within' gives the locus; جای 'place/seat' is the direct object; دانا 'the wise (one)' modifies جای; گزید is the past-tense verb 'chose' (گزیدن). Word order is typical Persian: indirect object (بدو) + locative (اندرون) + object (جای دانا) + verb (گزید).
segments.s659-g124: دل is the subject 'the heart'; از + مهر دانا = 'out of/for love of the wise one' (از marks cause/reason, مهر = love, دانا modifies مهر); بیکسو is an adverb 'to one side/aside'; کشید is the past form of کشیدن 'to draw/drag/turn' used figuratively here 'turned/leaned.' The clause means 'his heart, for love of the wise man, turned aside.'
segments.s659-g125: نبد (نبُد) is the negative copula 'was not' (classical/poetic form) applying to the following predicate; روزش 'his day' (روز + enclitic ش) is the subject of that negation; آرام 'calm/at ease' is the predicate. و شب جای خواب is an elliptical clause coordinated with the first: 'and night (was) no place for sleep' — جای خواب = 'place for sleep.' The negative (نبد) governs both coordinated ideas in this compact poetic phrasing.
segments.s659-g126: تنش 'his body' (تن + ش) and دلش 'his heart' are two parallel noun phrases describing his condition; پر ز سختی = 'full of hardship' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', سختی = 'hardship'); پرشتاب = 'full of haste' or 'very hurried' used predicatively. The clause is copular/attributive and elliptical: 'his body full of hardship, his heart full of haste.'
segments.s659-g127: چهارم is an ordinal/adverbial 'fourth' or 'for the fourth (time)'; چنین گفت is the phrase 'thus spoke/said' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said' — poetic speech marker); شاه جهان 'king of the world' is the speaker (title). The order is emphatic/poetic: ordinal + reported speech marker + subject.
segments.s659-g128: ابا denotes reluctance/hesitation (here 'with reluctance' or 'he hesitated'); پیشکارش 'his steward/attendant' (پیشکار + enclitic ش) is the indirect object; سخن درنهان 'words in secret' = 'he spoke privately' (سخن = speech, درنهان = 'in secrecy/in private' — a single adverbial phrase). Together they mean he, reluctantly, spoke secret words to his attendant.
segments.s659-g537: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'again' (دگر = 'again', باره = 'occasion/time'). پرسید is the past tense verb 'asked'; موبد is the subject placed after the verb in this poetic order. که is the complementizer introducing the subordinate clause that follows ('that/whether').
segments.s659-g538: ز شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'from/than other kings' (ز = 'from' in poetic register); دگرگونه 'different' modifies the following noun phrase; خواهد here expresses future/will ('will want/raise'); سپاه is the noun object 'army' — the verb+object order is typical in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s659-g539: کدامست is the interrogative 'which is it?'. وچون functions as 'and how/and in what way' linking the next question; بایدت is the modal 'it must/ought for you' with the clitic -ت marking 'for you' (so 'should suit you'); مرد جنگ is a nominal phrase 'man of war' = 'warrior' (مرد here is heroic 'man').
segments.s659-g540: ز مردان introduces selection 'from the men'; the adjectives that follow (شیرافگن تیز چنگ) are epithets describing those men — 'شیرافگن' is a heroic epithet ('lion-slayer' or 'lion-like'), تیز 'sharp/keen', and چنگ here means 'claw' (a metaphor for fierce fighters). The final question mark is implied.
segments.s659-g541: چنین داد پاسخ = 'he gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus/so', داد past 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). The conjunction/particle که introduces the content of the answer. جنگی modifies سوار: 'جنگی سوار' = 'a warlike/fighting rider' or 'a warrior-horseman'.
segments.s659-g542: نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'should not / must not' and is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. سیر is a predicative noun 'sated / satisfied'; آید (present/subjunctive/optative form of آمدن) gives 'come' in the sense 'come to be' or 'return'; از کارزار = 'from the battle'. Together: 'it should not be that he returns sated from battle.'
segments.s659-g543: همان ... همان is parallelism emphasizing identity: 'the same ... the same'. بزمش is بزم + possessive -ش 'his feast/banquet' (here used metaphorically); آید is present/future 'comes' or 'will be'; رزمگاه is 'battlefield' — the phrase asserts that his feast and battlefield are one and the same.
segments.s659-g544: به introduces an adverbial/time phrase ('in' or 'during'); رخشنده روز = 'radiant/bright day(s)'; و links the two temporal phrases; شبان سیاه is a poetic expression 'black nights' (شبان here is a poetic plural for 'nights' rather than the literal senses given in the lexicon); سیاه is the adjective 'black/dark' modifying شبان.
segments.s659-g17: برهنه شد (became naked/bare) is the past predicative phrase; the patient/subject بازوی او (his arm) appears after it, a typical classical word order where the verb precedes the subject. از جامه ('of/from the garment') is a genitive/ablative phrase attached to برهنه شد explaining what was removed — i.e. the arm was bared of its garment.
segments.s659-g18: یکی مرغ (a certain/a single bird) is an indefinite noun phrase fronted before the verb; رفت (went/flew) is the simple past. از هوا (from the air) gives the source and سوی او (toward him) the direction; overall a straightforward Subject–Verb–Adjunct order typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s659-g19: فرودآمد is a past-tense simple verb meaning 'descended'. از ابر ('from a cloud') is the source phrase, and مرغ سیاه (a black bird) is the subject phrase placed after the source — note the noun + adjective order (مرغ then سیاه) as in classical Persian.
segments.s659-g20: ز پرواز شد literally uses ز + the masdar پرواز with شد (became/ceased), conveying that it left or stopped its flight. تا introduces a limit/result clause 'until / as far as', so تا ببالین شاه means 'until it came to the king's bedside'; ببالین is the fused prepositional form (بـ + بالین) meaning 'to/at the bedside'.
segments.s659-g21: ببازو is an ablative/locative phrase 'by/at the arm' placed before the verb. نگه کرد is the compound verb (نگه + کرد) meaning 'held/looked/kept (watch)'; و links the two actions and بدید is simple past 'saw', with گوهر (gem) as its direct object. The construction is verb-centered with its objects/adjuncts on either side, typical of Shahnameh diction.
segments.s659-g22: کسی را به نزدیک او is the object phrase 'someone to near him' (کسی marked by را, and the preposition به indicating motion/toward). برندید here functions as a past-tense verb meaning 'brought' (classical variant of بردن), so the clause means 'someone was brought near him.' Note the object-before-verb order; the lexical entry given in the word list doesn't match this verbal use, so the sense is not listed there.
segments.s659-g23: همه لشکرش is 'all his army/host' (همه modifying لشکر with the possessive suffix -ش 'his'). گرد آن مرغزار means 'around that meadow' (گرد = around), so the whole noun phrase expresses that his entire host was around the meadow.
segments.s659-g24: همی‌گشت is the imperfective-past continuous form ('kept going/was going about'); the prefix همی- signals continuous action in classical Persian. هرکس ('each person/everyone') is the subject, and ز بهر شکار ('for the sake of the hunt') is a purpose phrase (ز بهر = for the purpose of), so the clause says that everyone was moving about in search of prey.
segments.s659-g25: همان شاه تنها بخواب اندرون is a nominal + predicate phrase describing the king's state. همان (demonstrative) emphasizes 'that/the very' king; شاه is the subject; تنها qualifies him as 'alone'. بخواب here functions as a narrative past participial/elliptical verb phrase meaning 'slept' (classical/poetic diction uses بخواب where modern Persian would have خوابید); اندرون is a locative adverb 'inside' placed after the verb.
segments.s659-g26: نه ... رهنمون is a negative existential construction: نه negates the existence of a رهنمون ('guide') around the king. بر گرد او is a prepositional phrase 'around him' (بر + گرد gives 'around/about'); برکسی functions as 'for anyone' (indefinite object), and رهنمون is the noun 'guide'. The clause states absence rather than performing an action (implicit copula/auxiliary is omitted in classical style).
segments.s659-g27: چومرغ سیه بند بازوی بدید begins with چُو (here written together as چومرغ) functioning as the temporal conjunction 'when' (literary usage). سیه‌بند is a compound adjective 'black-banded' modifying مرغ 'bird'; بازوی is the direct object 'the arm' (or 'arm/limb') that the bird saw; بدید is the past tense 'saw' (classical preterite form). Word order is temporal clause → subject → object → verb, typical of epic diction.
segments.s659-g28: سر درز آن گوهران بردرید is the action clause that follows: سر درز آن گوهران is the direct object phrase ('the heads/tops of those gems'—سر 'head/top' + درز 'seam/crack' forms a part/portion phrase), and بردرید is the strong past verb 'tore off' (بردرید = برد + برید, an older/periphrastic past meaning 'tore apart'). The agent (the bird) is implicit from the previous clause; the verb is third-person past.
segments.s659-g29: چوبدرید گوهر یکایک بخورد is an episodic past sequence. چوبدرید here reads as a literary/compound form meaning 'when (they) cut' (چو = 'when' + بدرید = 'they cut/tore'); گوهر یکایک 'each gem' is the subject/affected item (یکایک = 'one by one'); بخورد is used in the narrative past sense 'fell/ befell' (classical use of خوردن to indicate something happening to/for an object). The clause thus means 'when they cut, each gem fell.'
segments.s659-g30: همان در خوشاب و یاقوت زرد is an elliptic locative/result phrase describing where/thus the gems ended up. همان ('those/that same') refers back to the gems; در is the preposition 'in' or 'into'; خوشاب is a noun 'sweet/clear water' and is coordinated with یاقوت زرد 'yellow ruby' by و. The verb is omitted (understood from previous clause), which is common in poetry.
segments.s659-g31: بخورد و ز بالین او بر پرید is a two-part past narrative clause. بخورد is the past-tense verb 'it ate' or more generally 'it consumed/was taken', coordinating with و 'and'. ز بالین او 'from his pillow/bedside' is the source phrase (ز = 'from', بالین = 'pillow/bedside', او = 'his'), and بر پرید is the past verb 'flew up/away' (برپرید = 'flew off'). The subject (the bird or creature) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s659-g32: همانگه ز دیدار شد ناپدید is a short result clause: همانگه 'at that very moment' is a temporal adverb, ز دیدار literally 'from sight' (ز = 'from') and شد is the auxiliary/past 'became' or 'was'; ناپدید is the predicate adjective/participle 'invisible/vanished'. Together they mean 'at that moment it became unseen/vanished from sight.' Note دیدار here is used as a noun 'sight/appearance' rather than its usual sense 'meeting'.
segments.s659-g81: بدو گفت is a common classical phrasing meaning “he said to him”: بدو is a dative/indirect‑object pronoun placed before the verb for emphasis and گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’. کین بار is an archaic demonstrative + noun phrase (literally ‘this burden/charge’) with کین before بار; بر introduces the following NP as the locus/target (‘upon’/‘concerning’), here بر دستشوی = ‘with regard to the hand‑washer’ or ‘in the matter of the hand‑washer’. Word order is topical: indirect object + verb, then the headed noun phrase with preposition.
segments.s659-g82: This is an admonition addressed to تو (second‑person singular, here poetic/thou). با marks the instrument/means (‘with’), followed by آب‌جو as the instrumental noun. هیچ تندی مجوی is a negative imperative: مجوی is the negative‑imperative/present‑subjunctive form ‘do not seek’, تندی ‘haste’, and هیچ intensifies the negation (‘at all/any’). The verb appears at the end as usual for prohibitions.
segments.s659-g83: چولب را places the (obscure) noun چولب before the verb with را as the definite/object/topic marker; را here flags the NP that the verb affects. ببالاید is a classical third‑person present/optative form meaning ‘be proud / cause to be proud’ and is followed by an ablative/prepositional phrase از بوی خوش ‘because of the pleasant scent’, where از marks cause/reason and بوی خوش = ‘pleasant smell’.
segments.s659-g84: تو is the addressed second person (explicit for emphasis). از introduces a source/partitive phrase; ریخت here is a noun meaning the act/form of pouring or the stream/flow (context: the manner/pouring of the cupbearer), and آبدستان is the water‑attendant/cupbearer in genitive relation to ریخت. نکش is the negative imperative ‘do not draw back/withdraw’. The verb is final and the negative imperative directs the addressee not to pull away from that pouring.
segments.s659-g85: چو روز دگر is a temporal clause ‘when (on) the next day’; چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’, روز dger = ‘another/next day’. شاه نوشین‌روان is the head noun plus an epithet: نوشین‌روان is a descriptive epithet attached postpositively to شاه (‘the fair/sweet‑souled king’).
segments.s659-g86: بهنگام خوردن is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘at the time of eating’ (بهنگام = ‘at the time of’, خوردن = ‘eating’). بیاورد is the past/historical present of ‘bring’ (‘he brought’), and خوان is the direct object ‘banquet/feast’. The verb plus object thus mean ‘he brought the banquet’ timed by the preceding phrase.
segments.s659-g87: پرستنده را دل uses را after پرستنده to topicalize/mark the possessor (‘as for the worshipper’), followed by دل ‘heart’. پراندیشه is a compound adjective/nominative meaning ‘full of thought / pensive’, and گشت is the past copular/auxiliary ‘became’. Together: ‘the worshipper’s heart became pensive.’
segments.s659-g88: بدان here functions adverbially ‘thereupon / with that’ (formed from the prefix ب‑ + دان in the text); تا introduces a temporal clause ‘until’. دگر بار = ‘once more / again’, بنهاد is the classical past form of نَهَدن ‘to place’ (here ‘he set/placed’), and تشت is the object ‘basin’. The whole clause means roughly ‘thereupon, until he again set down the basin…’ (the verb بنهاد governs تشت).
segments.s659-g345: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دانا is the subject 'the wise one' (noun + adjective). رخ شاه پژمرده is the object phrase: رخ (face) with the possessor شاه (king) following it and the adjective پژمرده (withered/pale) qualifying رخ. یافت is the simple past verb 'found/observed' (here equivalent to 'saw') placed at the end of the clause in classical poetic order.
segments.s659-g346: روانش is روان ('soul') with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his'. به درد اندر is a prepositional phrase marking the state/condition ('into/with pain', اندر reinforcing 'in/within'). آزرده is an adjective 'distressed/afflicted' describing روانش. یافت again is the past verb 'found' — the clause means 'he found his soul distressed in pain.'
segments.s659-g347: برآورد is a past tense verbal form meaning 'brought forth' / 'took out' (here transitive). گوینده is the agent 'the speaker'. راز از نهفت is the object phrase 'the secret from concealment' with از marking source and نهفت ('hidden') qualifying راز. Word order is slightly inverted (verb before subject), typical of classical poetry.
segments.s659-g348: گذشته همه is a noun phrase 'all that has passed / the past, all of it' (گذشته as 'the past' and همه quantifying it). پیش کسری is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before Khosrow/Kisra' (پیش = in the presence of). بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said / he told' placed at the end to complete the sentence.
segments.s659-g349: ازان is a fused prepositional phrase 'from that' (از + آن) pointing to the preceding passage or stanza. بند here means 'stanza/passage' and is followed by a short enumerative list: بازوی 'the arm' (a noun that can also imply strength) and مرغ سیاه 'the black bird' (noun + adjective after it). This hemistich lists items without an explicit verb — the verb is supplied by the next hemistich.
segments.s659-g350: از here functions as 'concerning / of' (introducing topics). اندیشه گوهر is 'the thought/consideration of the gem' (اندیشه 'thought', گوهر 'gem' as the topic). و links the next noun phrase خواب شاه 'the king's dream' (خواب 'dream', شاه as possessor). Together the segment names further topics related to the stanza.
segments.s659-g351: بدو is the enclitic prepositional pronoun 'to him'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said', introducing reported speech. کاین begins the reported clause ('that this'), بودنی is a verbal adjective 'that-which-is / of being' and کار is 'a matter / affair'; بود is the copula 'was/is' — together كاین بودنی كار بود = 'that this is a matter of being' or loosely 'this is how things are.'
segments.s659-g352: ندارد is the present-tense negative 'has not / admits not' (literally 'does not have'). It governs the coordinated nouns پشیمانی و درد سود: پشیمانی 'regret' (so 'it admits no regret') and 'درد سود' is a compact phrase meaning 'pain has no profit' or 'suffering brings no benefit' — سود is 'benefit/profit'. The verb is used once and applies to both coordinated objects.
segments.s659-g529: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he came' (poetic simple past). به درگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the court/presences' with درگاه used for the royal court or sovereign's presence; the coordinator و links the two verb phrases. بنشست is another past-tense verb 'sat down' and مست is an adjectival predicate placed after the verb (typical in older Persian) meaning 'intoxicated/drunk'. The sequence shows two consecutive actions: he came and then sat (in a drunken state).
segments.s659-g530: همیشه ('always') is a sentence adverb setting habitual time. جز از here is the exception construction 'except (for)'; از is the preposition 'from/of' used with جز to mark what is excepted. می‌ندارد is a negative present/factual-form verb meaning 'does not have' (here describing habitual possession: he has nothing). به دست is a locative/agent phrase 'in the hand' (به + دست used idiomatically 'in the hand' or 'at hand'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic meter but reads: 'always, except for wine, he does not have (anything) in his hand.'
segments.s659-g531: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'among' introducing provenance or class. کارآگهان (plural of کارآگه) means 'the informed/knowing ones' and functions as a descriptive apposition; موبدی is the agent noun 'the mobad/priest' performing the speech. گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (used to introduce reported speech) and شاه at the end is the addressee/vocative 'O king' or indirect object 'to the king'—word order is inverted for emphasis/poetry (agent and qualifiers precede the verb and addressee).
segments.s659-g532: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. راند is a past tense verb 'he/you/they drove/led/put in motion' (here 'led' in the sense of leading forces). سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. جنگ is 'war' and قیصر names the opponent 'Caesar' (genitive relation: 'Caesar's war'). سپاه 'army/host' is the object that was led; poetic word order places سپاه at the end after the directional phrase. The whole segment reads: 'when he led the army toward Caesar's war.'
segments.s659-g533: نخواهد is the negative future/modal form 'will not' or 'does not/will not wish to' (here functioning as a negative future/modal: 'he will not ...'). جز marks exception 'except' and ایرانیان را is the definite object 'Iranians' marked by را (emphatic/definite object marker); به جنگ is the prepositional phrase 'to war'. Together this means 'he will (do) nothing except (send) Iranians to war' — the verb implied by context is understood (poetic ellipsis), so the clause restricts who will be taken to battle.
segments.s659-g534: جهان 'the world' is the semantic subject, شد is the past/resultive verb 'became', and به ایران is a dative/experiencer phrase 'for/with respect to Iran'. بر از روم تنگ is a comparative/agentive phrase: بر ... از can mark comparison or cause 'on account of/from Rome' and تنگ 'narrow'. The line therefore means 'the world became narrow/tight for Iran because of Rome' (i.e. Iran was constrained by Rome). Word order and prepositions are compressed for poetic rhythm.
segments.s659-g535: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave a reply' (چنین 'thus/so'; داد 'gave' — past tense; پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. آن دشمنی is the subject of the subordinate clause 'that enmity' (آن = that; دشمنی = enmity). The segment sets up reported content: 'thus he answered that that enmity...'.
segments.s659-g536: به طبع است uses به + noun + است to mean 'is by nature' or 'is natural' (به here has an adverbial/causal flavor 'by/according to'). طبع 'nature/temperament' is the predicate nominal and است is the copula 'is'. و links the second predicate: پرخاش آهرمنی is a compound noun phrase 'an Ahrimanic aggression/assault' (پرخاش 'attack/aggression' qualified by آهرمنی 'Ahrimanic, demonic'), so the whole sentence says 'it is natural and (is) an Ahrimanic aggression.'
segments.s659-g169: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when/since' introducing a cause or circumstance. با + گنج puts 'treasure' in an instrumental/associative relation ('with/compared to the treasure'). رنجش is a noun 'suffering' (here emotional pain) and برابر نبود is the negative past copula phrase 'was not equal' — literal order is 'when with-treasure suffering equal was-not' (subject omitted but understood).
segments.s659-g170: بفرسود is a past/inchoative verb meaning 'wasted/decayed' (he languished). ازان درد is a prepositional phrase 'from that pain' (ازان = from that), and و connects the clauses. در غم is another prepositional phrase 'in sorrow', modifying the final verb بسود, which here functions as a verbal predicate meaning 'was consumed/was wasted away' (poetic usage).
segments.s659-g171: چنان بد که is a standard correlative: 'so/such... that...' — چنان 'so', بد 'bad/grave', and که introduces the result clause. قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar'. ب and دان appear here as a fused/archaic sequence (بدان) meaning 'about that / thereupon' or 'in that matter'; the two characters are shown separately in the data but together mark relation to the previous clause. چندگاه is an adverb 'for a while/for some time' modifying the action that follows.
segments.s659-g172: رسولی here denotes an 'envoy' or 'messenger' (an object of dispatch). فرستاد is the past tense 'sent' (active, simple past). نزدیک شاه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to/near the king' specifying the recipient or the destination of the envoy.
segments.s659-g173: ابا (here orthographically variant of با) introduces the means/instrument: 'with'. نامه و هدیه و با نثار lists items sent as accompaniment: 'a letter and a gift and with offerings' — the conjunctions و connect the nouns in a standard coordinate list.
segments.s659-g174: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' specifying the following object. درج and قفلی are two nouns in apposition — 'a chest/drawer (درج) and a lock (قفلی)'. برو here is best read as a contracted/poetic form meaning 'بر او' (on it / upon it) or otherwise marking relation to استوار; استوار is an adjective 'firm, secure' describing how the lock/box is set — 'a single chest and a lock, firm upon it.'
segments.s659-g175: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause linking this description to the object just mentioned. با شاه 'with the king' indicates accompaniment or association. کنداوران و ردان are coordinated plural nouns naming groups associated with the king (literally 'those who dig/investigate' and 'those who drive/repel' — here generally 'investigators/excavators and attendants/agents').
segments.s659-g176: فراوان بود is a simple past copular clause 'were many/abundant'. پاکدل is an adjective 'pure‑hearted' qualifying the following noun, and موبدان is the plural noun 'mōbeds' (Zoroastrian priests) — so the clause states that pure‑hearted mobeds were numerous.
segments.s659-g113: Subject + verb followed by a poetic, inverted adjectival phrase. پرستنده is the subject ('a worshipper') and آمد is the simple past 'came'. The phrase پر از آب چهر literally orders as 'full of water face' (adjective + prepositional complement + noun) — a poetic inversion meaning 'his face was full of water'.
segments.s659-g114: بگفت is an archaic/classical past form meaning 'he said' (the b- prefix common in Ferdowsi). آن سخنها is the determiner + noun 'those/that words'. به marks the indirect object 'to/unto', so بوزرجمهر is the recipient 'to Bozorgmehr'.
segments.s659-g115: چنین 'thus' introduces the manner; داد (here 'gave') is the simple past verb and پاسخ is its direct object 'an answer/reply'. بدو ('to him') is the indirect object. نیکخواه, the agent 'the well-wisher', appears after the verb — an instance of inverted/poetic word order where the subject follows the verb.
segments.s659-g116: که introduces a reported/content clause (the content of the reply). روز من is the possessive noun phrase 'my day' (noun + possessive pronoun), followed by the comparative adjective آسانتر 'easier'. The comparison is marked by از ('than') before the standard of comparison روز شاه 'the king's day'.
segments.s659-g117: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. برگشت و آمد is a compound past narrative 'returned and came', describing swift movement; چو باد is a simile placed after the verbs meaning 'like the wind'.
segments.s659-g118: همه modifies پاسخش and means 'entirely/altogether'. پاسخش ('his/its answer') is the direct object. کرد is the simple past 'did/made' used here in the sense 'recounted' or 'related'. The phrase بر ... یاد (بر شاه یاد کردن) is a verbal construction meaning 'to make known to the king' or 'to tell the king', so بر marks the target (the king) and یاد is the noun used in this idiom.
segments.s659-g119: ز پاسـخ ('because of/from the answer') gives the cause. بر آشفت is a past verb phrase meaning 'was upset/angered' (بر often occurs with آشفتن). و شد 'and became' links to the following simile; چون پلنگ is 'like a leopard'. The sequence expresses reaction and transformation (emotion compared to an animal).
segments.s659-g120: ز آهن here indicates material/source ('of/from iron' i.e. made of iron). تنوری is 'an oven/furnace' (the object noun), and بفرمود is an archaic/honorific past form of فرمود meaning 'he ordered/commanded'. تنگ is the adjective/complement 'tight/narrow' that characterizes the furnace; the verb+adjective sequence means he commanded it to be made tight/narrow.
segments.s659-g49: که is the relative/conjunction 'who/that' introducing the clause. بس زود is an adverbial phrase (بس = emphatic 'very/quite', زود = 'soon/quickly') modifying دید (past 'saw'). آن نشان نشیب = 'that sign of decline' with نشان (sign/token) as head and نشیب (descent/decline) as its qualifier; word order is demonstrative + noun + modifier in this poetic, somewhat compressed line.
segments.s659-g50: خردمند is the subject 'the wise man'. خامش بماند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: خامش ('silent') + بماند (present/past-form of 'to remain') = 'remained silent' or 'fell silent'. از نهیب = 'at/from the rebuke' (از = 'from/at', نهیب = 'a loud rebuke'); this prepositional phrase gives the cause/manner of the silence.
segments.s659-g51: همه 'everyone/all' here quantifies the following scene; گرد بر گرد is a repetitive poetic phrase meaning 'around about' (گرد = 'around', بر used in the spatial sense), followed by آن مرغزار 'that meadow' as the locus. The repetition emphasizes encirclement of the meadow.
segments.s659-g52: سپه بود is a simple past-state clause 'an army was/stood'; و اندر میان شهریار links with 'and' to add that the شهریار (the king) was 'in the midst' (اندر میان) of that army. Classical/poetic word order places the location phrase (اندر میان) immediately before the noun شهریار.
segments.s659-g53: نشست here functions as a past action 'sat down' or in this idiom 'dismounted' (نشستن از بر اسب = to dismount from a horse). از بر اسب is the classical locative phrase 'from off the back of the horse'. کسری names Khosrow (Kisra) and is the possessor/owner of the horse. بخشم is an archaic/poetic formation meaning 'in anger' (ب + خشم) indicating the manner; it is not the modern verb 'to give' despite its surface form.
segments.s659-g54: ز ره = 'from the road' (ز is the poetic variant of از). تا marks the limit 'until' and در کاخ uses در in the sense 'door/gate of the palace' (here meaning 'palace entrance'). نگشاد چشم is a compact verbal phrase: نگشاد (negative/perfect form of گشاد 'open') with چشم 'eye(s)'—literally 'the eye did not open', i.e. 'he did not lift/turn his eyes to look'—the verb phrase gives the action continuing up to the stated limit.
segments.s659-g55: همه is the plural subject 'everyone'. ره ز دانا is a compact phrase: ره (here 'manner/way' or 'on the path/at the cue') + ز دانا ('from the wise one' or 'because of the wise one'); together they indicate that the action follows/comes from the wise man's influence. همی is the older continuous/emphatic particle. لب گزید is an idiom 'to press/ bite the lip' = 'to keep silent' or 'to restrain speech' (گزید here carries the sense 'to bite').
segments.s659-g56: فرود آمد is a compound verbal idea 'came down / alighted' (فرود = descent; آمد = came). از باره چندی is a temporal/occasional phrase 'after a short while' or 'after that occasion' (باره = occasion/instance; چندی = 'for a little while' / 'a short time'). ژکید is a terse poetic verb describing a negative change of state (withered, drooped, or became discomfited); the exact nuance is archaic and context-dependent, here signaling that the person fell into a dejected or ruined condition shortly after.
segments.s659-g409: به + توقیع here = 'by/in a written decree' (به used as an instrumental/locative preposition before توقیع). گفت is the narrative past 'he said' with the subject omitted (typical epic narration). آنچه is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which/those who' and is followed by the verb هستند and the predicate adjective خرد, giving 'those which are small.' Word order: relative pronoun → copula → predicate adjective.
segments.s659-g410: ز دست اسیران is a prepositional phrase 'from the hand(s) of the captives' (ز = 'from' in poetic register; دست = 'hand' meaning possession/control). نباید شمرد is an impersonal prohibition/negation: نباید + verb-stem (شمرد) = 'should/must not be counted/ reckoned.' The construction says these little ones are not to be counted as in the captives' possession.
segments.s659-g411: سوی + مادران‌شان = 'toward/to their mothers' (سوی = 'toward(s)'). فرستید here functions as an imperative/order (2nd person plural or narrative second-person form commonly used for commands in epic diction) meaning 'send (them)'. باز follows as an adverb 'back/again' indicating return.
segments.s659-g412: به دل شاد is a prepositional phrase 'with a happy/joyful heart' (به used as 'with' or 'in' idiomatically). وز is an archaic/poetic connective/particle (here functioning like 'and' / 'and free of') leading into خواسته بی‌نیاز, where خواسته = 'desire/wish' and بی‌نیاز = 'free from need' (i.e. satisfied). The phrase describes the state in which they are to be returned.
segments.s659-g413: نبشتند is the past tense 3rd person plural 'they wrote/recorded' (note: in classical usage this form appears where modern Persian would use نوشتند). کز introduces a reported/content clause meaning 'that/from which' → here 'that (from) Rome ...'. صد مایه‌ور is a numeric phrase 'a hundred (who are) wealthy' (مایه‌ور = 'wealthy'), functioning as the subject of the reported clause.
segments.s659-g414: همی is a modal particle that marks habitual/future expectation or emphasis; combined with the verb بازخرند (باز- + خرند) it yields 'they will buy back / will redeem.' خرند is 3rd person plural present/subjunctive form used here for an expected action. خویشان is the object 'relatives' and به زر is the instrument/means 'with gold.'
segments.s659-g415: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' باز خرند is the verb phrase 'they buy back / they redeem' (باز- prefix + خرند, 3rd person plural present/subjunctive/optative expressing the conditional action). گفت is the narrative 'he said' (introducing the reported instruction), and از هراس is an adverbial phrase 'out of fear' giving the motive for the following injunction.
segments.s659-g416: به هر مایه‌دار‌ی = 'from/to each wealthy person' (به + هر marks distribution 'for each'). یک مایه کاس is a quantitative phrase 'one mạye (a unit/measure) [in] a cup' — کاس = 'cup/bowl' used as a measure; syntactically it gives the amount to be taken from each rich man. Word order places the beneficiary/agent phrase (به هر...) before the quantity.
segments.s659-g217: این عبارت یک جملهٔ خبری شعرى است: 'یکی کار' = 'a certain affair', 'پیش آمدم' is a fixed idiom (پیش + آمدم) meaning 'befell me / came before me' with آمدم being first-person past. 'ناگزیر' is a comment/adjective ('inevitable' or 'unavoidable') qualifying the event; word order (quantifier + noun + verbal idiom + adjective) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s659-g218: 'کزان' = 'from which' (source/causal connector) introduces the effect; 'بسته' is a past-participial adjective (closed/bound) describing the state, placed before the verb 'آمد' (came/arrived) — together 'بسته آمد' = 'came bound/was bound (when it came)'. 'دل' is the affected noun ('heart') and 'تیزویر' is an obscure compound/name whose precise meaning is unclear here (treated as a single modifying word or name).
segments.s659-g219: 'یکی درج زرین' is a nominal phrase ('a certain / a certain one' + درج + adjectival زرین = 'a certain golden درج'); 'سرش' = 'سر + ـش' (noun 'top/lid' + enclitic pronoun 'its'); 'بسته' is a past-participle used adjectivally ('shut/closed') and 'خشک' here intensifies that closure (literally 'dry' but functioning as 'firm/tight' in context). The adjectival description ('سرش بسته خشک') precedes the verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s659-g220: 'نهاده' is a past participle / perfective verb meaning 'placed / set' and is the main verbal action of the line. 'برو' here functions as a prepositional cluster (poetic contraction of بر + او = 'on it'), so the phrase means 'placed on it'. The direct objects 'قفل و مهری' ('a lock and a seal') follow the verb, and 'ز مشک' is a prepositional phrase 'of/from musk' modifying مهری. (Note: مهری here = 'a seal' though the provided sense-list gives other senses.)
segments.s659-g221: 'فرستاد' is the past tense verb 'he sent' (placed before the subject in this poetic order); 'قیصر' is the subject 'Caesar'; 'بر ما' = 'to us' (indirect object) and 'ز روم' = 'from Rome' (source). The line follows a topical/poetic order: verb → subject → indirect object → source.
segments.s659-g222: 'یکی موبدی' = 'a certain mobad' (موبدی = Zoroastrian priest). 'نامبردار' is used attributively here (literally 'name-bearing' / 'renowned' or 'so-called'), introducing the following identifier 'بوم' which in context functions as the priest's name; in other words, 'a mobad named/renowned Bōm.'
segments.s659-g223: 'فرستاده' = 'the messenger' (subject); 'گوید' is present/reported-speech 'says' (reporting verb); 'که' is the complementizer 'that'; 'سالار' = 'the commander/chief' and 'گفت' = 'said' (introducing the quoted command). The sequence marks reported speech: messenger (reports) that the commander said ...
segments.s659-g224: This is the content clause of the reported speech: 'که این راز پیدا کنید از نهفت'. 'که' = 'that', 'این راز' = 'this secret' (direct object), 'پیدا کنید' is a second-person plural subjunctive/imperative ('find out / discover' — polite/commanding), and 'از نهفت' = 'from the hidden / out of concealment' (a prepositional phrase specifying source or manner).
segments.s659-g73: Literary word order: the phrase نگه سوی من ('a look toward me') appears before the subject بنده and the verb کرد. نگه here functions as 'a look' / 'to look' (the action), سوی من is the directional phrase 'toward me', بنده is the agent 'the servant', and کرد is the simple past 'did/turned (his look)'.
segments.s659-g74: که introduces a result/clause ('so that / so'), followed by the speaker's verb گفتم 'I said' (classical past). The phrase سرآمد ... خواب و خورد is an idiom: خواب خورد (or خوابم خورد) = 'sleep overtook me / sleep fell upon me.' وخورد is the perfect/past verbal element 'ate/overcame' used idiomatically; the clause reports what the speaker said.
segments.s659-g75: چون گشت here introduces a temporal/circumstantial clause 'when/as he became'; جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' and بامن is the dative/target 'toward me'. درشت is an adjective describing his manner ('rough/harsh'). Word order is typical of classical Persian: subject + temporal clause + predicate adjective.
segments.s659-g76: مراسست شد = 'became/showed courtesy' (مراسست 'courtesy/attention' with شد as 'became/was shown'). The logical subject is آبدستان 'the water‑attendant / cupbearer'; بمشت is the dative 'to me'. Together this says the cupbearer became attentive/polite toward me.
segments.s659-g77: بدو is the indirect object 'to him'; دانشی is an epithet 'the learned one' (agent). گفت is the reporting verb 'said'. The sequence آب آر خیز contains imperatives: آر (archaic verb 'bring') and خیز 'rise/get up'. In classical text verbs and imperatives can appear directly after the reporting verb.
segments.s659-g78: چنان چون که is a fixed emphatic phrase 'in such a way that / just so' introducing the manner of the command. بر دست شاه is the prepositional phrase 'on the king's hand', and آب ریز is an imperative 'pour water' (ریز here functions as a verb/command). The clause specifies how the water should be presented.
segments.s659-g79: بیاورد is the past-form 'he brought' (here simple past), followed by the subject مَرد جوان 'a young man'. آب گرم is the object 'warm water'. Classical Persian commonly places the verb before its full subject phrase.
segments.s659-g80: همی‌ریخت is an imperfective/past-continuous form 'was pouring' (همی‑ prefix marks continuous/aspectual action). بر دست او = 'on his hand' (prepositional phrase), and the reduplicated نرم نرم is an adverbial repetition meaning 'softly/gently' (emphatic adverb).
segments.s659-g585: ستمکاره را is the direct object phrase: ستمکاره (the oppressor) + را (definite object marker). زنده modifies the object and means "alive". بر دار is a prepositional + noun phrase meaning "on/upon the gallows" (بر = on/upon, دار = gallows). کن is the imperative "do / make" addressed to someone; together زنده بر دار کن = "hang (him) alive on the gallows." Word order is object + verbal phrase, a typical Persian imperative construction where the object (with را) precedes the verb.
segments.s659-g586: دو پایش = دو (two) + پای (feet) + ـش (his) — a possessed noun phrase 'his two feet.' زبر here is an adverbial/locative sense meaning 'upward/above' (archaic/poetic). سر نگونسار کن is an object + adjective + imperative: سر (head) + نگونسار (downcast/bowed) + کن (imperative 'make'); literally 'make his head downcast.' The two clauses are coordinated instructions expanding how to hang him.
segments.s659-g587: بدان is the imperative 'know' built from the prefix ب- + دان (know); here ب functions as the classical verbal prefix and دان is the root. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that.' کس از پهلوانان ما = 'anyone of our champions' (کس = anyone; از is the partitive/of preposition; پهلوانان ما = our heroes/champions). The whole segment sets up a purpose clause.
segments.s659-g588: نپیچد is negative + present/subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person singular of پیچیدن (ن- negator + پیچد), here rendered as 'may not turn/stray.' دل و جان is a paired noun phrase 'heart and life' (common poetic collocation). ز پیمان ما uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) meaning 'from / away from' and پیمان ما = 'our pledge/covenant.' Together the clause expresses a wish/prohibition that no one's heart or life turn away from the covenant.
segments.s659-g589: دگر here is an adverb meaning 'again' / 'once more.' گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd person singular) — the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense, so the sense index is -1. کای is the archaic/vocative particle 'O' used to address someone; شاه یزدان‌پرست is the addressee 'king, God‑worshipper' (شاه = king; یزدان‌پرست = religious/pious or God‑worshipper). The whole segment is a speech report: 'Again he said, “O pious king.”'
segments.s659-g590: This short clause places (an action or address) toward a group: بدر is here treated as a proper name or epithet (the sense list gives 'full moon' or 'proper name' — in this context it functions as a name/epithet). بر is the preposition 'toward / to' (directional use). بسی functions as a quantifier 'many / a great number of' (classical/poetic use). مردم زیردست = 'people/subjects who are under (his) authority' (زیردست = subordinates/subjects). Grammatically it marks the addressee group to whom the utterance or action is directed.
segments.s659-g591: همی‌داد is an archaic/perfective-imperfective verbal form meaning 'kept giving' or 'caused (continually)' — here it functions causatively: 'he had/made (them) do X.' او را is the pronominal object 'him' with را marking definiteness. ستایش کنند is an infinitival/subordinate clause with کنند (subjunctive/optative 'they should do' or 'that they do') forming 'that they praise.' Together the segment means 'he caused them to praise him' or 'they continually praised him (by his direction).'
segments.s659-g592: جهان‌آفرین را = 'the world‑creator' (i.e., the Creator) with را marking object; نیایش کنند is parallel to the previous verb phrase (نیایش = prayer/worship; کنند = they do / subjunctive), forming 'that they pray to/worship the Creator.' The two coordinated verb phrases describe actions he caused or that were continually done.
segments.s659-g545: نگردد is a negative jussive/subjunctive (3rd person) of گشتن used as a wish/command: 'may it not become' or 'let it not be'. بهنگام is a temporal adverbial 'at the time/at the crucial moment'. نیروش is نیرو + the bound pronoun -ش (his/its force/army) and is followed by the adjective کم ('small/few'), so the noun precedes its adjective as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s659-g546: ز here means 'from/through/regardless of' in a causal or concessive sense ('whether from...'). بسیار and اندک contrast 'much/abundance' and 'little/scarcity'. نباشد is again the negative 3rd‑person subjunctive of بودن, functioning as a jussive 'may (he) not be'; دژم is the predicate adjective 'sullen/grim'. The phrase thus says 'may he not be sullen because of much or little.'
segments.s659-g547: دگر گفت = 'he said again' or 'again (one) said'; دگر is an adverb/particle 'again' here. گفت is the past of گفتن ('said') used without an explicit subject (typical narrative omission). کای is a vocative interjection 'O', introducing direct address, followed by شاه 'king' and the epithet نوشین‌روان 'sweet‑souled' in apposition.
segments.s659-g548: This is a direct wish spoken to the king: همیشه 'always' sets the scope; بزی is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person jussive/imperative 'may you live' (the verbal form addresses the king). شاد and روشن‌روان are coordinated adjectival predicates 'happy and bright‑souled' describing the wished‑for state. The implied subject is the vocative addressee (شاه) from the previous segment.
segments.s659-g549: بدر is used as a poetic noun 'full moon' (often an epithet for beauty) and بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain', followed by مرد بد 'a bad/evil man' (noun + adjective). از نسا means 'with regard to women' or 'of women' (نسا = women, poetic plural), i.e. 'bad where women are concerned' or 'a man bad about/with women.'
segments.s659-g550: پرستنده here means 'admirer/one who worships' (i.e. lover/adorer) and کاردار is an adjective/noun meaning 'capable/experienced' or 'skilled one'. بسا functions as an adverb/existential 'many' or 'there were many' modifying the preceding nouns; the phrase says that many were admirers and many were capable (followers/men).
segments.s659-g551: درم 'dirhams' is the noun 'coins/money'. ماند is the past 'remained/was left'. بر وی = 'for/on him' (i.e. 'remained to him'). سیصد هزار is the numeral 'three hundred thousand' giving the amount; Persian word order in poetry can place numerals after the verb phrase.
segments.s659-g552: به دیوان is a locative/benefactive phrase 'in/to the diwan' — here 'diwan' means the register/official record. چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. کردند is the past plural of کردن 'they did/made'. با او شمار literally 'counted with him' or 'reckoned him' — شمار here is 'count/tally' and the clause describes entering or tallying the sum in the official register.
segments.s659-g289: اگر introduces a conditional (‘if’). تیره شد is a simple past (‘became dim’) with شد as the past copula/verb; the noun چشم follows poetically after the verb (inversion of the more prosaic چشم تیره شد). دل روشن است is a balanced main clause: دل (subject) + روشن (predicate adjective) + است (copula). The contrast between dim eye and bright heart is typical poetic antithesis.
segments.s659-g290: روان را places the soul in topical/emphatic position with را (here functioning more as topic/emphasis than a straightforward object marker). ز دانش ('from/by knowledge') gives the cause or means. همی is the archaic/poetic continuous particle meaning roughly ‘always/indeed’ and precedes the noun جوشن (armor) which functions metaphorically as a predicate; است is the copula. The whole means the soul, by knowledge, is (like) a protective cuirass.
segments.s659-g291: ز گفتار او is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase (‘from/because of his speech/words’). شاد شد is the past tense ‘became happy’; note the poetic order where the verb phrase (شاد شد) precedes the subject شهریار (verb–subject inversion) rather than the more prosaic شهریار شاد شد.
segments.s659-g292: دلش combines دل + enclitic possessive ش (‘his heart’). تازه شد is past ‘became fresh/new’. چون گل اندر بهار is a simile: چون = ‘like/as’, گل = ‘a flower’, اندر = archaic/poetic preposition ‘in’, بهار = ‘spring’. The phrase paints the heart’s renewal by likening it to a flower in spring.
segments.s659-g293: ز اندیشه (z andīshe) is a prepositional phrase 'because of / from thought' placed at the start for cause. شد is the simple past 'became' (classical Persian past third singular). شاه را uses the accusative/topic marker را to mark the person affected (the experiencer) — in this construction Persian treats the person whose posture changed as a marked object. پشت راست is a noun + adjective ('back straight') with adjective following noun; together پشت راست شد = 'his/ the back became straight' (i.e. he sat up straight).
segments.s659-g294: فرستاده و درج را پیش خواست is a single clause where فرستاده و درج را is the coordinated object (the messenger and the 'darj'/document) marked by را. پیش here means 'before / in the presence of' (brought forward). خواست is the past tense 'wanted/caused' used idiomatically as 'summoned' or 'had (them) brought forward' — classical Persian often uses خواست in this sense.
segments.s659-g295: همه موبدان is a quantified noun phrase 'all the mobeds' (همه before the noun). وردان را is the definite object 'the chants/recitations' marked with را. بخواند is past third singular of خواندن used here causatively/imperatively-narrative to mean 'he caused them to recite' or 'he had them recite' — in epic narration this form reports that he ordered or made the priests recite.
segments.s659-g296: بسی دانشی is an adverbial/quantified noun phrase 'much/great learning' (بسی here = 'much / abundant'). پیش دانا places that learning 'before / in the presence of the wise (man)' — پیش = 'in front of / before' and دانا = 'the wise one'. نشاند is an archaic past verb meaning 'set/placed/caused to sit'; here it means 'set before' or 'presented to' (i.e. he displayed or placed much learning before the sage).
segments.s659-g433: همه مردگان را is a definite object phrase (همه 'all' + مردگان 'the dead') marked by را as the direct object of the verb کند. کند is the present/past narrative form of کردن used as 'make/cause'; بیش (literally 'more') modifies یاد (remembrance), so کند بیش یاد = 'causes the remembrance to be greater' — a compact, somewhat inverted poetic order (object before verb, and the modifier بیش preceding the noun یاد).
segments.s659-g434: پر از غم شود is a predicate 'becomes full of grief' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of/from', غم = 'grief', شود = 'becomes'). The following phrase زنده را جان شاد is inverted: زنده را ('to the living', with را marking the affected party) + جان شاد ('the life/soul [is] glad'), where the copula is omitted as typical in Persian poetry and شاد is the adjectival predicate modifying جان.
segments.s659-g435: چنین داد پاسخ is 'gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus/such', داد 'gave' in past, پاسخ 'answer' as object). The conjunction که introduces the content clause that follows. از مرده یاد begins the subordinate clause ('remembrance/of the dead'): از here marks the relation 'of/about' in the idiom از مرده یاد کردن ('to remember/mention the dead'), and the clause's verb is postponed to the next segment (poetic suspension).
segments.s659-g436: کند is the finite verb completing the subordinate clause (یاد کند = 'remembers' / 'makes mention of'). The relative/indefinite subject is هرک ('whoever'); دارد خرد با نژاد is a nominal phrase 'has wisdom with/and noble lineage' where دارد is present 'has', خرد = 'wisdom', and با نژاد = 'with lineage' (i.e., of good birth) — together: 'whoever has wisdom and noble birth remembers the dead.' Note the verb-before-subject placement is a poetic inversion.
segments.s659-g437: هرآنکس که = 'whoever' introducing the conditional relative clause. از مردگان دل بشست is literal 'washed his heart from the dead' (دل بشست = 'washed/cleansed the heart', از مردگان = 'of/from the dead'), a figurative expression meaning 'has purged himself of concern for the dead' or 'has ceased to remember them.' بشست is a past tense verb 'washed'.
segments.s659-g438: نباشد ورا is a negative existential/predicative construction: نباشد ('will not be' / 'is not') + ورا ('to him' / 'for him'), so 'will not be for him.' نیکویها درست = 'good things properly' (نیکویها plural of 'good/excellent things', درست = 'proper/right'); together the clause says such a person will not properly obtain/receive good things. The ordering is compressed and idiomatic — 'نباشد ورا نیکویها درست' = 'good things will not be rightly for him.'
segments.s659-g439: یکی گفت = 'someone said' (یکی 'a certain one/someone', گفت past of گفتن 'said'). کای شاه is a vocative address 'O king' (کای as poetic/vocative particle, شاه 'king'). کهتر پسر is a noun phrase 'the lesser/younger son' (کهتر 'lesser/inferior', پسر 'son') placed after the vocative as the topic of the reported speech.
segments.s659-g440: نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive form 'will not become/will not turn' (from گردیدن/گشتن). همی is an adverbial particle adding emphasis ('still/indeed/just'). گرد here is used in a compact, poetic sense tied to 'turn/turning/turn of fortune' and داد پدر literally 'the father's giving' (داد can be 'gift/grant' or 'gave'); read together as a compressed assertion that the father's gift/turn will not come to the younger son. The line uses elliptical, archaic ordering typical of epic verse, so several words collocate to express 'the father's grant/turn will not be (given) to him.'
segments.s659-g457: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed formulation meaning “gave such an answer / answered thus.” داد is the past of دادن “to give” used with پاسخ “answer.” The conjunction که introduces the reported content; ما را خرد is an inverted possessive/experiential expression meaning “our wisdom” (ما را marks the experiencer/possessor and خرد is the thing that follows as the subject of the subordinate clause).
segments.s659-g458: ز here marks cause/ori gin (“from / because of / at the sight of”), followed by دیدار “seeing / sight.” ایشان is the third-person plural pronoun “them.” همی‌بگذرد is a classical imperfective/present-form of گذشتن with the prefix همی- (habitual/continuous flavour) meaning “passes / deserts” — the grammatical subject is خرد (wisdom) and ما را is its experiencer/possessor.
segments.s659-g459: A coordination of qualities: هش (here a noun meaning alertness/heed), دانش “knowledge,” and رای “counsel/opinion” form the compound subject. دستور ماست is the predicate with copula ست ‘is’ — literally “are the rule of ours.” The word order places the qualities before the copula, typical of Persian nominal sentences.
segments.s659-g460: Another nominal clause in parallel: زمین گنج = “the earth [is] treasure” (noun + appositive), و links to اندیشه گنجور ماست = “and thought is our treasury.” گنجور here is an adjectival/nominal form meaning “treasury / treasure-holding,” and the final ماست is the copula ‘is (ours).’
segments.s659-g461: دگر + گفت = “again said” (دگر = again). گفت is the past-tense verb “said” introducing direct speech. تو‌ای شهریار is a vocative: تو + ای (vocative particle) addressing the king (شهریار). Word order is emphatic/poetic: the adverbial/temporal دگر precedes the verb.
segments.s659-g462: عقابی is indefinite (the -ی can make a noun ‘an eagle’); گرفته‌ست is a perfect/resultative form (گرفته + ست) meaning “has taken / has been taken / has been seized” in third-person singular. روز شکار is a genitive/nominal compound “day of hunting” (adverbial/time phrase). Together they report the happening: an eagle has been taken — it is the hunting day.
segments.s659-g463: چنین گفت = “thus said he” (reporting speech). کاو را is an object phrase: کاو is an older/poetic form meaning ‘him/that man’ and را marks it as the object. بکوبید is a classical verbal form (بـ- prefix + کوبید) that can function as an imperative or an optative/subjunctive in reported speech, here meaning “strike” (i.e. command). پشت is the direct object “back” (i.e. “strike him on the back”).
segments.s659-g464: که introduces the explanatory/reason clause “because / that.” با مهتر خود is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward/with his chief/superior” (مهتر = chief/elder; خود = himself/his own). چرا شد درشت is a simple predicate: چرا (why) + شد (became) + درشت (rude/insolent), i.e. “why did he become insolent.” The clause gives the reason for the commanded punishment.
segments.s659-g577: چنین داد پاسخ is an idiomatic past reporting phrase: چنین 'thus' precedes داد (past of دادن, 'gave') and پاسخ ('answer') to mean 'he answered thus'. The conjunction که immediately follows to introduce the content clause (the thing said), and آن خواسته = 'that request/demand' is the head of that clause.
segments.s659-g578: This is a relative clause introduced by که modifying آن خواسته: 'که از شهر مردم کند کاسته' = 'which would lessen the people's number from the city'. از marks source/'from', مردم 'people' follows the place شهر 'city', and the verbal phrase کند کاسته uses کند (present/optative 3rd sg of کردن) with the participial/past form کاسته to express potential/result ('will/might cause to be diminished').
