segments.s659-g399: دگر is an adverb here meaning 'again'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (the lexeme list gives noun senses for گفت; its use here is the verbal past 'said' — reported speech follows). انوشه is the speaker's name (subject). بدی جاودان is the reported clause 'evil (is) eternal' with the copula omitted, bad/evil (بدی) followed by the predicate adjective جاودان.
segments.s659-g400: نشست is past 'he sat down' (or 'took a seat'). The sequence 'نشست و خور و خواب' is a paratactic list of activities: نشست (sat), خور (here the present-stem/nominal action 'eating' / poetically 'ate'), و خواب (and sleep). با موبدان is the prepositional phrase 'with the mobeds' (Zoroastrian priests), indicating company.
segments.s659-g633: درم (coin) precedes the verb بستد (past 'closed/locked up'), following an object-before-verb (SOV) tendency in Persian poetry: 'he shut up the money'. از بلخ is a simple prepositional phrase 'from Balkh', and بامی here is locative ('on a roof' / 'of a roof') attached to that phrase; به‌رنج is an adverbial phrase 'with toil/through hardship' modifying the action. The whole segment is a compact clause describing how/where the money was secured.
segments.s659-g634: سپرده functions as a noun 'the deposit / entrusted thing' and is the theme that follows the verb نهادند (past of نهادن 'to place, to set'); together سپرده نهادند = 'they placed/deposited'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the verb, and به گنج is the goal 'into the treasury' (به = 'into/towards', گنج = 'treasure'). Word order is typical Persian: [object] [verb] with adverbial/PP complements.
segments.s659-g635: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'gave such an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the reported content. ما را درم is a dative-like construction ('for us, money…') where را marks the affected party (literary dative/experiencer), so the following predicate will say that 'money is not needed for us.'
segments.s659-g636: نباید که باشد is a negated subjunctive/jussive construction meaning 'it should not be / there should not be'. کسی زُو دژم = 'anyone hostile toward him'; ز + او (classical contraction written as زو) = 'from him / toward him'. دژم here means 'sullen/hostile'. The clause therefore reads 'there should be no one who is hostile toward him.'
segments.s659-g637: که introduces a content/explanatory clause 'that/because'. رنج آید is present/subjunctive-style 'suffering comes / arises'. از بیشی گنج ما is a genitive phrase 'from the excess/surplus of our treasure' (بیشی = 'excess', گنج = 'treasure', ما = 'our'), with Persian word order placing modifiers before the head.
segments.s659-g638: نه چونین بود is a negated copular phrase 'was not like this' (نه = not, چونین = thus/such, بود = was). داد از پادشا = 'the justice/from the justice of the king' (داد = 'justice' here, از = 'from/of', پادشا = 'the king'), so the whole clause means 'the king's justice was not of that sort.' Note the poetic inversion: the predicate and genitive phrase are arranged compactly.
segments.s659-g639: از آنکس که بستد بدو = 'to/for that person who closed/secured it for him' (از introduces 'to/from that person' in the sense of allocation), with a relative clause introduced by که. بستد is past 'shut/secured', بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him/for him'. هم دهید is an imperative/jussive 'give also' (دهید = 2pl/polite imperative or subjunctive used as a command). The segment issues the instruction to give to that person as well.
segments.s659-g640: ز گنج آنچ خواهد = 'from the treasury whatever he wants' (ز = from, گنج = treasury, آنچ = 'that which/whatever', خواهد = 'wants/desires'). بران سر نهید is an idiomatic/jussive sequence: بران 'upon that' or 'over it', سر 'head/chief' (metaphorical = a person in charge), نهید (from نهادن) 'place/set' — together 'appoint a head over it.' Notation and tense are jussive/imperative directives.
segments.s659-g609: This is a conditional clause introduced by اگر (if). بازگیریم is a first-person-plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'we take back / we reclaim' (classical usage often uses present-subjunctive for conditional actions). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and ارزانیان is a plural agent noun 'the stingy / the cheap ones' (objects from whom something is taken).
segments.s659-g610: This is the main clause: همه modifies سود (here 'altogether/all of the profit'), فرجام is an adverbial/noun 'in the end / at the outcome', گردد is the 3rd-person-singular present-subjunctive of گشت/گرد 'become / turn into', and زیان is the predicate nominal 'loss'. Word order places the temporal/adverbial فرجام before the verb and the resulting predicate (زیان) at the end.
segments.s659-g611: دگر here means 'another' (i.e. another speaker) and is followed by گفت, the past verb 'said' (classical narrative order: subject/adjunct + گفت + direct speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O', شهریار 'king', and بلند is an epithet 'lofty / exalted' attached to the vocative.
segments.s659-g612: که introduces the content of the speech ('that' / 'that ... may ...'). هرگز + مبادا form a strong negative wish ('may it never be' / 'may no ... ever ...'). به marks the target 'to/against', جانت is 'your life/being' (possessed), and گزند is the noun 'harm'. The clause is therefore an optative/negative wish: 'may no harm befall your life.'
segments.s659-g613: This is a nominal sentence: جهودان و ترسا ('Jews and Christians / pious people') form a compound plural subject. تو را uses the object marker را with 'you' to mark you as the one affected, and دشمنند is the predicate 'are enemies' — here دشمن + the 3rd-person-plural copula ending (‑ند) yields 'are enemies'.
segments.s659-g614: دو رویند literally 'they are two-faced' (دو + روی(ه) + plural/predicate ending) — an idiom for being duplicitous. و is the conjunction 'and', با marks association 'with / in (their) creed', کیش means 'creed / sect', and آهرمنند is a predicate meaning 'they are of Ahriman / demonic' (Ahriman = the evil principle). The clause condemns their character and religious allegiance.
segments.s659-g615: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact phrase 'thus gave an answer / thus replied' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the reported content; شاه سترگ is a nominal phrase 'the king, the lofty/large one' (شاه = king, سترگ an epithet). Note classical word order: report phrase then 'که' then the content clause.
segments.s659-g616: This subordinate clause names the person and states the negative assertion: ابی زینهاری appears as a proper-name/epithet (here taken together as the king's name or title), نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive 'is not' (3rd person), and بزرگ is the predicate 'great'. Together they state 'Abi Zinehari is not great.' In classical Persian the negated copula + predicate follows the named subject.
segments.s659-g497: 'که' introduces a relative clause ('that/which'). 'آن چیز' is the head noun 'that thing.' 'کاو' is an archaic combined relative (literally 'که او' = 'that which/which he'), here linking the head noun to the verb phrase. 'خود' is a reflexive/emphatic 'himself.' 'کند' is the 3rd‑person present/past (classical narrative) of 'کردن' used here as an auxiliary/main verb 'does/makes' and attaches to the following noun 'آرزوی' to mean 'he wishes' or 'he causes desire.' The whole segment reads literally 'that thing which he himself makes (his) wish.'
segments.s659-g498: 'سپارد' is the verb from 'سپردن' meaning 'to deliver/leave' or here 'to cast/throw away' (3rd person). 'همه کاسه' is the direct object 'all the bowls/cups.' 'بر چار سوی' is a prepositional phrase 'toward the four directions/sides' with 'بر' = 'toward/to', 'چار' = 'four', and 'سوی' = 'direction(s)'. Word order is verb + object + directional phrase; the omitted subject is understood from context.
segments.s659-g499: This compact clause stacks verbs and arguments in a classical style. 'نبوید' is negative + form of 'بوییدن' ('to smell') used impersonally here ('it is/will not be smelled' or 'no scent appears'). 'نیازد' (from 'نیازیدن') is 3rd‑person present meaning 'he begs/supplicates' or 'needs'; it takes 'بدو' ('to him') as indirect object. 'نیز' = 'also' modifies the following nominal 'دست' ('hand'), which is left to connect to the next verb. The clause therefore sets up two related actions: the impersonal negative about smelling and the act of supplication toward 'him', with 'نیز دست' linking into the next clause.
segments.s659-g500: 'بلرزد' is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of 'لرزیدن' meaning 'trembles' or 'will tremble.' 'دل' is the noun 'heart' (here emotional center). 'مرد' is 'man/person' (can mean 'hero' in epic but here general 'man'). 'خسرو‌پرست' is a compound adjective/noun 'king‑worshipper' or 'royalist' modifying 'مرد.' The phrase reads literally 'the heart of the king‑worshipping man trembles,' and grammatically the verb at the start agrees with that subject; it also can be taken as the continuation of 'نیز دست' (also the hand trembles) by close connection in the poem's terse style.
segments.s659-g501: 'چنین داد پاسخ' is a fixed classical formula: 'gave such an answer' (چنین = 'thus/such', داد = past 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). The following 'که' introduces the content of the answer. 'از بیش خورد' literally 'from/through excess befell' — here 'از' marks source/cause, 'بیش' = 'more/excess', and 'خورد' is the past form of 'خوردن' used in the sense 'befell/was suffered' (not 'to eat'), so the clause means 'he suffered more' or 'greater harm befell him.'
segments.s659-g502: 'مگر' here functions as a conditional 'unless.' 'آرزو' is the subject 'wish/desire.' 'بازگردد' is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of 'بازگشتن' 'to return.' The prepositional 'به درد' ('to pain/sorrow') gives the result/state: 'unless the desire returns to pain/sorrow.' Grammatically this is a protasis (conditional) introduced by مگر that qualifies the preceding clause.
segments.s659-g503: 'دگر گفت' = 'another (one) said' (دگر = 'other/another', گفت = 'said' here), a speech-introducing phrase. 'هرکس نکوهش کند' is a generic subjunctive/relative clause: 'whoever (هرکس) censures/reproaches (نکوهش کند)'. 'کند' is the present/subjunctive form 'does/might do' used in generic relative statements. The whole segment introduces an objection: 'another said: whoever reproaches...'.
segments.s659-g504: 'شهنشاه را' is the direct object 'the emperor/king' marked by the object marker 'را'. 'چون' here is interrogative 'how' (not 'like' or 'because'), introducing the question. 'پژوهش کند' literally 'does investigation/searches' (پژوهش = 'investigation/research', کند = 'does/will do'); together 'چون پژوهش کند' asks 'how will he investigate/examine him?' Grammatically the clause queries the feasibility or manner of investigating the king.
segments.s659-g441: بریزد is a classical/historic-present (third‑person) verb form from بریختن/بریزدن meaning 'to pour/throw'; همی is an adverbial particle adding continuous or habitual sense ('keeps/continually'); بر زمین is a locative prepositional phrase 'onto the ground'; بر درم is the object expressed as a prepositional phrase with بر + درم (archaic/poetic word order: the coin is expressed with بر after the locative).
segments.s659-g442: که introduces the subordinate clause giving reason ('because' here); باشد is the subjunctive/optative/present of بودن used as 'is/may be'; فروشنده is the noun 'seller'; او is the third‑person pronoun 'he/she' referring to that seller; دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' predicated of the seller.
segments.s659-g443: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact SOV classical phrasing 'thus gave (an) answer' (داد = gave, here a simple past); که introduces reported content ('that'); این نارواست is the reported clause with ناروا (unjust/improper) as predicate and ست the archaic copula ending (equal to است).
segments.s659-g444: بهای زمین is a genitive compound 'the price (بهای) of the land (زمین)'; هم here is an emphatic/adversative particle meaning 'indeed/also' (stressing the statement); فروشنده is the possessor 'the seller' and راست is the predicate adjective 'right/just' meaning 'it rightly belongs to the seller' (copular sense expressed by راست at clause end).
segments.s659-g445: دگر گفت = 'another said' (دگر = another/other; گفت here stands for 'said' in the narrative); کای is a classical vocative particle 'O'; شاه is the addressee 'king'; برتر‌منش is an epithet 'high‑minded' used vocatively with the king.
segments.s659-g446: که introduces the exhortation/subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that' / 'that you should'); دوری is a noun 'avoidance/distance' used verbally here as an admonition 'keep away'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; بیغاره is an older/obscure noun (here denoting a shameful or discreditable state — the exact nuance is archaic); و links the next noun; سرزنش is 'reproach/blame', so the phrase means 'keep away from بیغاره and reproach.'
segments.s659-g447: دلی داشتی is past tense 'you had a heart' (دلی = 'a heart' / heartfelt disposition; داشتی = second‑person past 'you had'); پیش ازین is a temporal adverbial 'before this' or 'formerly' (پیش = before; این in older orthography written ین); پر ز شرم is a descriptive phrase 'full of shame' (پر = full (of); ز = of/from; شرم = shame), with the genitive introduced by ز in poetic style.
segments.s659-g448: چرا شد is the interrogative 'why did it become' (چرا = why; شد = became); برین سان is an older spelling of بر این سان 'in this manner/so' (بر + این + سان), here meaning 'so' or 'thus'; بی‌آزرم is a negative adjective 'without shame' = 'shameless' (بی‑ prefix), and و گرم coordinates a second adjective 'and ardent/zealous' (گرم used figuratively 'fervent').
segments.s659-g625: چنین داد پاسخ که is a short reporting clause: چنین (thus) modifies داد پاسخ (gave an answer). داد is the verb 'gave' with پاسخ as its object, and که introduces the reported content (that/which follows). دست فراخ begins the following clause and is a nominative noun phrase ('a generous/ample hand') that will serve as the subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s659-g626: همی ... کند is an archaic/poetic present habitual construction: همی (continuous/iterative particle) + کند (3sg present 'makes/does') = 'regularly/can make'. مرد را is the object marked by را ('a man' as the affected person), and نو is the adjective 'new/fresh' modifying the direct object یال و شاخ ('mane and horn(s)'). The subject 'دست فراخ' from the previous segment is understood here.
segments.s659-g627: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' followed by the temporal/conjunctive چون + گشت (past 'became/turned') giving 'when/when he became'. یزدان‌پرست is the predicate complement 'God-worshipper / pious'. Here چون functions as a temporal 'when/as' introducing the circumstance.
segments.s659-g628: نیازد here is used in the sense 'to need/seek' (classical usage). The phrase به‌بد ('for bad/for evil') is adverbial, در جهان 'in the world' locates the action, and نیز 'also/likewise' links to the previous idea; دست ('hand') is a nominal referring to the hand/power (here the giving/acting hand) and appears postverbally as the theme of the clause. The whole clause says the hand (i.e. the ruler's acting hand) no longer seeks evil in the world.
segments.s659-g629: جهان تنگ دیدیم literally 'we saw/found the world narrow': دیدیم is the past 1st‑person plural 'we saw/found', جهان 'the world' is the theme, and تنگ 'narrow' is the predicate adjective; بر تنگخوی means 'with respect to / for the narrow‑hearted (stingy)', with بر marking the target/affected group.
segments.s659-g630: This is a verbless predicative clause with inverted order: مرا (for me) + [آز و زفتی] (greed and filth) + نبد آرزوی (is not a desire). نبد is the older negative copula 'is not / does not exist as', and آرزوی is the predicate noun 'a wish/desire'. The whole means 'greed and dirt are not a wish/desire of mine.'
segments.s659-g631: چنین گفت موبد is the reporting clause 'thus said the mobad/priest': چنین 'thus', گفت (here reported speech verb 'said'), and موبد 'the mobad/priest'. که‌ای شهریار is a vocative address: که‌ای (archaic O/you) + شهریار 'king'. The sequence introduces the direct address to the sovereign.
segments.s659-g632: فراخان سالار سیصد هزار are epithets in apposition addressing the king: فراخان 'bountiful/generous' qualifies سالار 'chief/commander', and the numeral سیصد هزار 'three hundred thousand' quantifies the force or retinue (genitive relation understood). The phrase is a string of titles in vocative position.
segments.s659-g193: ازان پس = از آن پس, a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that' + پس = 'after'). بران داستا‌ن links the demonstrative to the noun: بران = بر + آن = 'on/at that', so بران داستان = 'at/on that tale'. خیره ماند is a compound verb: خیره (astonished/staring) + ماند (past of ماندن 'remain/be left'), meaning 'remained astonished' or 'stood staring'. The subject is implied (they).
segments.s659-g194: بزرگان و فرزانگانرا is the coordinated object with the enclitic object marker را (so: 'the great ones and the wise, (as object)'). بخواند is a past/preterite third-person singular (classical/poetic) of خواندن; here it means 'he summoned/called' (not 'read'), taking بزرگان و فرزانگانرا as its object.
segments.s659-g195: نگه کرد is a light verb construction: نگه (from نگه‌داشتن 'to hold/keep') + کرد (past of کردن) = 'kept/held back'. هریک = 'each (one)'. زهر باره‌ای is a compound: زهر 'poison' used figuratively ('venomous, malicious') + باره‌ای (here 'a single instance/shot/load') = 'a venomous thing/utterance' that one holds back.
segments.s659-g196: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/so that'. سازد is present/subjunctive/past- narrative form of ساختن here meaning 'may make/bring about' (i.e., 'might contrive'). مر is the enclitic archaic dative/accusative 'me' (to/for me). آن بند را = 'that bond/knot' with را marking the definite object. چاره‌ای = 'a remedy/way' in the indefinite accusative form; the clause means 'that (it) may make for me a remedy for that bond.'
segments.s659-g197: بدان is a fused prepositional phrase (ب + دان/آن) meaning 'to/at that' or 'with that'. درج means 'inscription/entry/line' here ('that inscription/line'), و = 'and', قفلی = 'a lock', چنان = 'so/such', and بی‌کلید = 'without a key' (compound with بی- 'without' + کلید 'key'). Together: 'with that inscription and a lock such as to be keyless.'
segments.s659-g198: نگه کرد again = 'kept/held' (i.e., he left/kept it in that state), و = 'and', هر = 'every/each', موبدی = 'mobed' (Zoroastrian priest), بنگرید is past/formal-subjunctive of نگریستن used as past narrative 'looked/gazed'. The clause reports that every priest looked (at it).
segments.s659-g199: ز دانش = 'from knowledge' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'). سراسر = 'entirely/throughout'; بیکسو شدند is a compound: بیکسو (to one side/aside; here 'at a loss/turned away') + شدند (past auxiliary 'became'), so 'they became wholly at a loss/turned aside from knowledge.'
segments.s659-g200: بنادانی here refers to 'the builders/establishers' (a common poetic term for learned craftsmen or founders — often used metaphorically for learned men). خویش is the reflexive/possessive formed from خوی + ش (so 'their own/themselves'), and خستو شدند = 'became weary/tired' (خستو = weary, شدند = 'became'). So: 'the builders themselves became weary.'
segments.s659-g249: خردمند بینا forms a two-word descriptive subject (literally 'wise, clear-sighted'); Persian permits adjective-like words following the noun or placed together as here. به + دانا marks the indirect object ('to the sage') and بگفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the b- prefix is the older/poetic past marker). Word order is subject — indirect object — verb for emphasis.
segments.s659-g250: سخن هرچ is an indefinite noun phrase 'whatever speech/words'; هرچ = 'whatever'. بر چشم او uses بر with چشم to mean 'in/for his sight' or 'to his eye'. بد نهفت is a poetic adjective/compound where نهفت (lit. 'hidden') with بد describes how the words presented themselves — 'appeared/lay as bad' in his sight. The verb is elliptically adjectival here rather than a full finite clause.
segments.s659-g251: چنین گفت is the fixed sequence 'thus said'; پرسنده را is the definite/direct object marked by را ('to the questioner'), while راه‌جوی is the subject ('the seeker') placed after the object for stylistic emphasis. Note: گفت here functions as the verb 'said' in the narrative.
segments.s659-g252: که introduces the reported content/purpose. بپژوه is an exhortative/subjunctive form of 'پژوهیدن' ('investigate/examine') addressing the seeker/listener. تا introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that / to see whether' and دارد + این ماه شوی forms the embedded clause meaning 'he/it has/ will become this moon' — ماه used metaphorically for a beautiful one and شوی (become) expresses the coming-to-be ('will become').
segments.s659-g253: زن پاکدامن is the subject noun phrase 'the chaste woman' (adjective following the noun). به پرسنده marks the indirect object 'to the questioner' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. Word order is straightforward subject — indirect object — verb.
segments.s659-g254: که introduces the reported content. شوی‌ست is a poetic fused form of شوی + است conveying 'will be/ is to be' (a prediction/statement of condition). و هم = 'and also'. کودک اندر نهفت is a verbless/descriptive clause: کودک 'a child', اندر 'within', نهفت 'hidden/latent' — together 'a child is concealed within (her)', i.e. she carries a child in her womb.
segments.s659-g255: چو = 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. داننده is the subject 'the knower/expert' and گفتار زن is the genitive phrase 'the woman's speech/words' functioning as object of hearing. The clause is a simple temporal subordinate ('when he heard...').
segments.s659-g256: بخندید is the past tense 'laughed' (here 'laughed at' someone). بر marks the target of the laughter ('at' or 'upon'), باره in this context functions as 'on the occasion/with respect to' or as a small connective, and گامزن is the noun 'the marcher/one walking by' — together: 'he laughed at the marcher/passant.'
segments.s659-g241: به آب خرد is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with/in the water of wisdom' (به + noun as instrumental/metaphorical). چشم دل را is a marked definite object: چشم دل = 'the eye of the heart' (an idiom for inner sight) and را is the direct‑object marker. بشست is 3rd‑person past singular (classical past form) meaning 'he washed'.
segments.s659-g242: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/among' introducing a source or cause; دانندگان is the plural agent/people 'the knowers/the wise'. استواری is an abstract noun 'steadfastness/firmness' and functions as the complement indicating the manner/state. بجست is an older past form 'he leapt/rose' (ب‑ prefix on past stem) meaning 'sprang up' rather than 'sought' in this context.
segments.s659-g243: بدو گفت is the common narrative sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said'); the following clause is reported speech: بازار من is the subject 'my market' or public place, and خیره گشت is a compound verbal phrase where خیره ('stunned/astonished') is predicative and گشت is the past 'became'.
segments.s659-g244: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; چشمم is 'my eye' (چشم + enclitic ـم), written here as separate parts in the word list; ازین = از + این 'from/because of these', with رنج‌ها 'the sufferings/hardships' as the cause; تیره گشت is 'became dark' (تیره = dark, گشت = became).
segments.s659-g245: نگه کن is an imperative 'keep watch' or 'look!' (نگه = gaze/watch, کن = imperative 'do'). The first که introduces the subordinate/relative clause; پیشت 'before you/in your presence' is adverbial; the second که is the relative 'who/that' and آید is present/subjunctive 'comes'. به راه 'on/to the road' modifies آید. The clause as a whole means 'be on the lookout for anyone who comes upon the road before you.'
segments.s659-g246: ز here means 'about/concerning' (poetic 'from' used with an object) and حالش is 'his state/condition' (حال + possessive ـش). بپرس is the imperative 'ask!' (addressing the addressee). ایچ is an emphatic negative 'at all/nothing', نامش is 'his name' (نام + ـش), and مخواه is the negative imperative 'do not ask/seek'. Together: 'Ask about his condition; do not ask his name at all.'
segments.s659-g247: به راه آمد is a simple past narrative 'came/went onto the road' (به = toward/onto, راه = road, آمد = came). از خانه بوزرجمهر places the origin 'from the house of Bozorgmehr' (از = from, خانه = house, بوزرجمهر = proper name). Word order is typical: verb with directional phrase followed by origin.
segments.s659-g248: همی‌رفت is the classical imperfect/continuous past 'was going' or 'kept going' (همی‑ prefix marks ongoing action). پویان functions adverbially/adjectivally 'moving/active/brisk', modifying the action; زنی is an indefinite noun 'a woman', and خوب‌چهر is a compound adjective 'fair‑faced/beautiful of countenance' placed after the noun (typical of older Persian poetic phrasing).
segments.s659-g281: This is a prepositional + verb + subject ordering common in classical Persian: 'به شاه جهان' (to the king of the world) marks the indirect object/recipient, 'گفت' is the reporting verb ('said') in the past, and 'بوزرجمهر' (Bozorgmehr) follows as the speaker/agent. The whole phrase means 'Bozorgmehr said to the king of the world.'
segments.s659-g282: 'که' introduces the reported content ('that' / 'who'); 'تابان' is an adjective 'radiant/shining' modifying the (implied) subject; 'بدی' here is an archaic/poetic verbal form (not the noun 'evil') functioning as a past/participial copula meaning roughly 'you were' or 'you shone'; 'تا' (= 'until') links a result/duration clause; 'بتابد' is from تابیدن 'to shine' (present/subjunctive/optative feel in poetry) and 'سپهر' is the sky/firmament. Together: 'that you were radiant until the sky shone.'
segments.s659-g283: 'یکی' is an indefinite determiner 'a certain/one'; 'انجمن' is the noun 'assembly/gathering'; 'درج' here is a noun in close apposition (classical/poetic usage) often 'inscription/entry' but functions as a modifier in the phrase; 'در پیش شاه' is a locative phrase meaning 'in the presence of the king' or 'before the king.' Grammatically, the phrase names a single assembly located before the sovereign.
segments.s659-g284: 'به پیش' = 'in the presence of' / 'before'; 'بزرگان' is the plural 'the great ones' or elders; 'جوینده راه' is a compound noun phrase 'seekers of the way' that qualifies or apposes the elders. Word order places the place/scene first ('in the presence of the great ones') followed by the descriptive phrase.
segments.s659-g285: 'به نیروی یزدان' is instrumental/ablative: 'by the power of God' (note the use of به with the instrumental sense). 'که' introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who'; 'اندیشه داد' is a verb phrase 'gave/ bestow(ed) thought' with 'داد' the past tense 'gave.' The clause qualifies the divine power: 'by God's power, who gave thought.'
segments.s659-g286: 'روان مرا' = 'my soul' (classical Persian uses the object-form pronoun 'مرا' in a genitive/possessive sense); 'راستی پیشه داد' is a transitive clause: 'gave (داد) raasti (uprightness/truth) as a پیشه (craft/practice).' Grammatically the verb 'داد' takes 'راستی پیشه' as the direct object and 'روان مرا' marks the beneficiary/possessed thing ('my soul').
segments.s659-g287: 'بگویم' is first-person present/subjunctive ('I will tell' / 'let me tell') used as a performative introductory verb; 'به درج اندرون' ('to/in the inner inscription') is the locative phrase pointing to where the content is; 'هرچ هست' = 'whatever is (there)' — 'هرچ' is an indefinite pronoun 'whatever' and 'هست' is the copula/existential 'is.' The clause announces intention to disclose whatever is written inside.
segments.s659-g288: 'نسایم' is an archaic/poetic first-person negative form (n‑ + verb) meaning 'I will not touch / I will not lay (my hand) on'; 'بران' = 'upon that / on it'; 'قفل' = 'lock'; 'و آن درج دست' = 'and that inscription [with my] hand' where 'دست' (hand) completes the idea of touching. Together the clause denies touching the lock or inscription with the speaker's hand.
segments.s659-g145: و گرنه که is a conditional/contrastive opening: و (and) + گرنه (otherwise / if not) + که introducing the following clause. دژخیم is the subject (an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'torturer' or 'executioner'). با تیغ تیز is an instrumental prepositional phrase (با = 'with', تیغ = 'sword', تیز = adjective 'sharp' placed after the noun in poetic order). The whole segment sets up the agent and instrument of the threatened action.
segments.s659-g146: نماید is a 3rd-person singular present/past-form used here as a potential/performative verb 'may show / will show / make (someone) experience'. تو را is the object with the Arabic-Persian object marker را following the pronoun (you). گردش رستخیز is the verbal/complement noun phrase: گردش 'turn, circuit' + رستخیز 'Resurrection' (rising/uprising); word order is poetic (verb before its full object phrase). The sense is that the torturer will cause you to 'see/undergo' the Resurrection's turning.
segments.s659-g147: The first که begins a reported clause ('that'); گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (here introducing reported content). The inner که again introduces the content quoted: زندان به از تخت شاه is an archaic comparative construction: X به از Y = 'X is better than Y', so 'prison is better than the king's throne.' Persian omits a copula; word order is compact: noun (زندان) + comparative phrase + the thing compared to (تخت شاه).
segments.s659-g148: This is an appositive/explicative noun phrase describing what 'prison' might be: تنوری 'an oven' (here a torture‑image) + پر از 'full of' (پر followed by از) + میخ 'nails'. با بند و چاه lists additional horrors with با 'with' + بند 'fetters/chain' and و چاه 'and a pit' (چاه = pit/trap). The phrase is literal and vivid; word order is standard noun phrase followed by complements.
segments.s659-g149: بیامد is past 'he came' (simple narrative). بگفت is an archaic/perfective form 'he told' or 'he said' (ب- prefix seen in older poetic diction). آنچ is a contracted form of آن‌چه 'that which / what', and بشنید is past 'he heard' — together آنچ بشنید مرد = 'what the man heard' (poetic word order places the relative phrase words compactly). Overall: 'He came and told what the man had heard.'
segments.s659-g150: شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became'. از درد is a causal/prepositional phrase 'from/by (the) pain' (از = 'from/of'). دانا is a predicate adjective 'wise', so شد از درد دانا = 'he became wise from/through pain'. دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ـش 'his'), and پر ز درد = 'full of pain' where ز is the poetic variant of از ('of'/'from'); the construction shows cause and psychological state in parallel clauses.
segments.s659-g151: بدان is the preposition be/به + demonstrative آن ('to that') in fused poetic form, functioning as an indirect-object marker 'to that (one)'. پاکدل is the addressee 'pure‑hearted (one)' (vocative or dative). گفت is the past 'he said' and بوزرجمهر is the speaker's name (Bozorgmehr) — word order here places the indirect object before the verb and the speaker after the verb, a normal poetic inversion: 'To that pure‑hearted one Bozorgmehr said...'.
segments.s659-g152: که introduces the content of what was said. ننمود is the negative past (ن + نمود) 'did not show / did not display' (here applied impersonally to fortune). هرگز is the adverb 'never'. بمابخت is the fused prepositional phrase ب + ما بخت = 'to/for our fortune' (i.e. 'to our luck'), and چهر 'face' means 'face/appearance' or idiomatically 'favour' — together meaning 'fortune never showed its face to us' (i.e. luck never smiled on us). The negation and adverbial element precede the object for emphasis.
segments.s659-g361: This is a nominal sentence where شکارست = شکار است (archaic/poetic contraction of 'is hunting'), so the copular idea is carried by -ست. The phrase کار شهنشاه is a possessive/genitive idea ('the work/task of the shahanshah') and رزم is paralleled to it with و; thus: 'The emperor's work is hunting, and (also) war.'
segments.s659-g362: A continuation/enumeration that supplies items governed by the copula of the previous segment; nouns are linked by coordinating و. می here is the classical word for wine (written mei/mi), شادی = rejoicing/celebration, بخشش = liberality/granting, داد = justice, بزم = feast/banquet. There is no new verb because these are additional predicates/items of the king's concerns.
segments.s659-g363: بداند is a jussive/optative 3rd-person form (literary: 'let him know' / 'he should know') introducing a subordinate clause with که. The clause که شاهان چه کردند پیش places the finite verb کردند at the end (typical Persian word order) and پیش = 'before' marks temporal reference.
segments.s659-g364: بورزد is another jussive/optative 3rd-person form meaning 'let him practise / apply / make use of'. Here it takes a following prepositional/demonstrative phrase—ب + دان (written together as بدان) meaning 'to that / of that / by that'—so بورزد بدان = 'let him apply that'. هم is an emphatic 'also/indeed', and نشان رای خویش is a genitive construction 'a mark/sign of his counsel' (خویش functions as the reflexive possessive 'his own').
segments.s659-g365: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' marking cause. آگندن (here in a causative/active sense) names the damaging action (to stain/defile/bring about); it governs the following coordinated nouns گنج و رنج سپاه = 'treasure and the army's suffering' (رنج سپاه = 'the army's hardship'). The whole phrase expresses a causal source: 'from (this) plundering/afflicting of treasure and army...'.
segments.s659-g366: Again ز introduces cause 'from'. آزرم here is an abstract or archaic noun (context suggests 'vainglory/arrogance' or a similar notion used as a source), followed by گفتار 'speech/boast'. وز in older Persian can function as a fused sequence meaning 'and from' (و + ز), linking to دادخواه 'one who seeks justice' or 'the plaintiff/complainer'. Together the segment lists further consequences: 'from pride/arrogance, boastful words and the complaints of those seeking justice.'
segments.s659-g367: دل و جان is a paired noun phrase 'heart and life/soul'. دستور باشد is jussive: دستور = 'order/discipline' and باشد = 'let it be' (optative), so the clause means 'let heart and life be set/ordered (so)'. به رنج is a prepositional phrase marking the condition/target: 'to hardship/to toil.'
segments.s659-g368: ز here carries a causal/contrastive sense 'from / away from / not for' introducing motives to be avoided: اندیشه کدخدایی = 'the thought/ambition of being a petty village-head (kəkhdāʾī)' and گنج = 'treasure'. The coordination with و links the two forbidden/undesirable motives.
segments.s659-g185: برین گونه is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'in this manner / thus' (بر + این گونه). دارم is the 1st-person present (used here with an almost future/performative sense) meaning 'I have' or 'I hold'; ز + قیصر = 'from Caesar' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'), and پیام = 'message'. Word order is typical older Persian: modifier (برین گونه) before the verb phrase دارم and the source phrase ز قیصر پیام as the object.
segments.s659-g186: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (poetic/thou). پاسخ گزار here is an imperative/hortative construction: گزار functions as 'report/tell/give' (imperative-like), so 'پاسخ گزار' = 'give an answer / answer'. آنچ = 'that which' (relative/indefinite 'what'), آیدت is literally 'comes to you' (3rd-person present with clitic -ت 'to you'), and کام = 'desire' or 'what pleases you'. The whole clause means 'you answer whatever comes to/pleases you.'
segments.s659-g187: فرستاده راگفت is an object-marked verbal phrase: فرستاده (the messenger) is the direct object marked by the enclitic را attached to گفت (here written راگفت), and شاه جهان is the subject 'the king of the world' (title). The literal order here is Object+را+Verb then Subject, a common classical word order; overall: 'the king of the world said to the messenger.'
segments.s659-g188: که introduces a reported clause 'that'. این هم 'this also/too' is the topic. نباشد is the classical negative present/subjunctive of بودن used here assertively to mean 'is not' (literary usage rather than an imperative), ز is the poetic 'from', یزدان = 'God', نهان = 'hidden/secret'. Together: 'that this too is not hidden from God.'
segments.s659-g189: من is the subject 'I'. از + فر = 'from (his) far/farr' (فر = royal divine glory); او is the 3rd-person clitic 'his/that of him'. این بجای آورم uses بجای 'in place of / instead' and آورم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form of آوردن used to indicate intention or future ('I will bring' / 'I shall bring'). The phrase means 'I will bring this in place of his farr.'
segments.s659-g190: همان مرد = 'that same man' (همان as demonstrative 'that/the same'). پاکیزه رای pairs an adjective 'پاکیزه' (literally 'pure/clean/chaste') with رای 'counsel/opinion' to describe the man's character as having 'pure/upright counsel.' آورم repeats the 1st-person verb 'I will bring.' The clause thus states 'I will bring that very man of pure counsel.'
segments.s659-g191: یکی هفته = 'one week' (duration). ایدر is an archaic form meaning 'here'. ز + می‌شاد = 'from wine he would rejoice' (می‌شاد is an imperfect/optative form meaning 'would be glad / rejoice'); باش is the 2nd/3rd-person hortative/imperative 'be' (let him be). The sequence gives a hortative instruction: 'Let him stay here one week, rejoicing with wine.'
segments.s659-g192: برامش is a prepositional/possessive cluster ('for him' / 'on his behalf' in older orthography), دل آرای is an expression where آرای (lit. 'ornaments' / 'adornments') functions to mean 'please/beautify the heart' or 'delight the heart', و آزاد باش is an imperative phrase 'and be free' or 'be generous/liberated.' Overall the clause is an exhortation: 'Delight his heart for him, and be free.'
segments.s659-g177: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'به + این' (here 'in/at/with this') prefixed as a single unit; the phrase names the location: 'قفل' (lock) و 'این درج' (and this inscription). نابرده دست is a compound adjectival phrase 'نـا-بردهِ دست' = 'not taken by hand' (untouched), placed after the nouns it qualifies. Word order is noun(s) + postpositive adjective phrase, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s659-g178: نهفته is a past-participle/adjective 'hidden'. بگویند is the verbal form 'they say' (used here in the sense 'it is said / they call'), introducing reported speech; because the provided lemma senses do not include 'say', mark it unavailable. چیزی که هست is a relative clause 'something that exists / that is'—چیزی (something) modified by 'که هست' ('that is' / 'that exists').
segments.s659-g179: فرستیم is 1st-person-plural of فرستادن here used in a conditional clause ('if we send'); ار is the conditional particle 'if' (poetic). باژ is the noun 'cry/shout' (the thing sent). بگویند is 'they say' / 'they will say' (reported speech, present/subjunctive plural) and راست is an adverb/adjective 'true/truly' (they will speak truthfully).
segments.s659-g180: جز از = 'except from / save for'; باژ repeats 'cry'; چیزی که آیین ماست is 'something which is our custom/rite' with آیین ماست as a predicate ('is our custom'), where ست is the poetic form of است ('is'). The whole phrase restricts the previous clause: 'except for the cry that is our practice.'
segments.s659-g181: گرای دون is a noun phrase: گرای 'inclination/leaning' qualified by دون 'base/low'. که introduces a relative clause 'which / that'; زین دانش is a terse poetic form of 'از این دانش' ('by/through this knowledge/learning'); ناگزیر here means 'compelled/forced' (passive sense). The clause thus says 'a base inclination which was compelled by this learning...'.
segments.s659-g182: بماند is the verb 'remained' (3rd-sg); the verb precedes its subject for poetic inversion. دل موبد تیزویر is the subject phrase 'the heart of the mobed Tizvir' (موبد = mōbed, a Zoroastrian priest; تیزویر is a proper name). So: '...remained in the heart of the mobed Tizvir.'
segments.s659-g183: نباید is the impersonal modal 'it must not / one should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'; خواهد is 3rd-sg of خواستن ('he/she/it wants' / 'would wish'). ز ما functions as 'on our account / because of us' (poetic 'ز' = از). باژ شاه here groups as 'the king's cry/issue' or more generally 'the king' in the clause; word order is compressed and poetic, so the exact attachment is flexible but the sense is 'one should not want that, because of us, the king ...'.
segments.s659-g184: نراند is the negative past/subjunctive form of راندن 'to drive away' ('drive off / expel'). The following phrase بدین پادشاهی سپاه is a compressed oblique expression: بدین (به + این) 'from/against this' or 'in this' + پادشاهی 'kingship, realm' with سپاه 'the army' as the direct object — together yielding 'drive the army from this kingship/realm.' Poetic word order places the verb before the locative phrase and object.
segments.s659-g137: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. برگشت is a past verb 'returned'. The clause follows a poetic SOV tendency: the verb phrase برگشت و پاسخ بیاورد ('returned and brought an answer') comes before the explicit subject مرد, which here functions as 'the man (who)...'. بیاورد is a past action 'brought/gave' rather than a literal present tense.
segments.s659-g138: ز گفتار is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of the speech/words'. شد is the copular/causative 'became' and introduces the resulting state. شاه را روی زرد is an idiomatic construction: شاه را marks the affected person (the king) and روی زرد ('face pale') is the resulting predicate — literally 'to the king a pale face became' = 'the king's face turned pale'.
segments.s659-g139: ز ایوان is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the iwan/portico'. یکی راستگوی is an indefinite phrase 'a certain truthful (man)', and گزید is the past verb 'chose'. Word order is classical/poetic SOV: location → NP → verb (گزید).
segments.s659-g140: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'راستگوی'. گفتار دانا is 'wise speech' (an attributive noun phrase). بداند is a present/subjunctive form 'would/should know' or 'can recognize', and شنید ('hears') completes the idea: in classical Persian the ordering here puts the recognizing verb before the sensory verb, so the clause means 'who knows/recognizes wise speech when he hears it.'
segments.s659-g141: ابا او here is a fixed, somewhat archaic/poetic expression meaning roughly 'with him' or 'beside him' (the manuscript/usage is older than modern colloquial); the exact sense is not one of the provided sense options. یکی مرد شمشیر زن is 'a certain man, sword‑striker' — شمشیرزن is a compound meaning 'swordsman' formed from شمشیر (sword) + زن (striker).
segments.s659-g142: که introduces a relative clause modifying that swordsman. دژخیم بود is a plain past statement 'he was a dzhakhim' (džakhim = torturer/oppressor), and اندران انجمن is a locative phrase 'in/among that assembly' (اندران = 'among them/therein', انجمن = 'assembly').
segments.s659-g143: که here introduces reported speech or an imperative 'say that...'. رو تو is the poetic ordering for 'your face' (modern روی تو). بد نهان ('badly/ill-hidden') modifies that face; ب (prefix) + دین in the manuscript combine into بدین here ('in/with this' or 'thus') but the provided lemma senses do not match the poetic clitic split, so دین cannot be assigned a listed sense reliably. را marks the following object/topic, and بگوی is the verb 'tell/say' (classical form). Overall this segment issues the instruction to conceal the speaker's ill aspect/face.
segments.s659-g144: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. پاسخت را is 'your answer' (پاسخ + possessive -ت), with را as the object/definiteness marker. بود is the copula 'were/was' (here conditional/hypothetical: 'if there were'), and رنگ و بوی literally 'color and scent' is an idiomatic way to say 'any sign or trace' — i.e., 'if your answer shows any sign.'
segments.s659-g265: که‌ای (که ای) is an archaic/vocative interrogative construction: 'O who...' or simply 'who are...'. خوب‌رخ is a compound adjective/noun 'fair‑faced / beautiful‑faced' placed next to the vocative. کیست is the copula 'is' (old poetic word order places interrogative elements around the copula). انباز is an archaic/obscure epithet here appended before the vocative تو; its precise meaning is uncertain in the text (poetic/obsolete), but functionally it qualifies the addressee. تو is the vocative 'you' (poetic register).
segments.s659-g266: برین is best read as بر این 'on/with this' (demonstrative phrase). کش here is not the literal verb 'to pull' but the agentive/qualifying element (‑کش) that combines with the following verbal noun to form a descriptive phrase — roughly 'that which draws/that drawing…'. خرامیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'strutting, to stride/strut' used as a noun for gait. و links the two coordinated nouns, and ناز تو is 'your coquetry/air' with تو marking the person whose coquettishness it is.
segments.s659-g267: مرا گفت = 'she/he said to me' (مرا = to me, preceding the verb in standard Persian object‑before‑verb order in poetry). هرگز is the negative adverb 'never' placed before the verbal complex. نبوده‌ست is the perfect negative 'has/had not been' and شوی is the second‑person form ('you be/you have been') used as the subject of that perfect — a poetic/inverted way of saying 'you have never been'.
segments.s659-g268: نخواهم is a negative present/future 'I will not/would not want' used here to introduce a subordinate clause. که introduces the content clause ('that' / 'that I ...'). پیدا کنم is a subjunctive construction 'I find/see' (پیدا as verbal/adjectival stem + کنم as 1st‑person subjunctive), with نیز 'also/anymore' modifying the action and روی 'face' the object (literal: 'I will not wish that I find also the face' → 'I will not wish to see your face again').
segments.s659-g269: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید (past) 'he heard' follows the conjunction, then the name بوزرجمهر appears (poetic order places verb before subject/object at times). این سخن 'this speech/these words' is the object (this phrase is what he heard).
segments.s659-g270: نگر is an imperative 'look!' or 'see!'. تا here introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that' and leads into the interrogative clause چه اندیشه افگند بن: چه = 'what', اندیشه = 'thought', افگند = past 'cast/threw', and بن = 'base/foundation' (metaphorical locus). Together: 'Look to see what thought he cast upon the foundation.'
segments.s659-g271: بیامد is the past 'came' (verb at the start – common in epic word order). دژم‌روی 'grim‑faced / sullen of visage' modifies تازان (here 'raiders' or fierce men). به راه = 'on the road' (به + راه indicating direction/location). The adjective precedes the noun in poetic ordering.
segments.s659-g272: چو = 'when' (temporal subordinate). بردند is the past 'they took/carried'. جوینده را is the object phrase 'the seeker' with را marking definiteness/accusative. نزد شاه = 'to the presence of the king' (نزد = near/in the presence of, شاه = king).

sentences.s659-s149: After that the king said to the messenger, "Deliver the message and demand an answer."
sentences.s659-s150: When the Roman heard, he opened his tongue and recalled all of Caesar's words.
sentences.s659-s151: He said that of the sovereign, victorious in battle, wisdom is necessary — and learning, renown, and (nang).
sentences.s659-s152: You possess the royal glory and exalted rank of a sovereign — greatness, wisdom, and the might of the hand.
sentences.s659-s209: Sell (them) and ask no more, for we need nothing from them for anything.
sentences.s659-s210: By the sword we will take jewels from them — those same (things): badreh, barde, silver and gold.
sentences.s659-s211: They said that among the city's wealthy there are two merchants who, twice each night, ...
sentences.s659-s212: One of them cannot bring his head into sleep because of the singing of the revelers and the harp and the rebab.
sentences.s659-s169: All the mobeds cried out in praise; with that knowledge they scattered jewels.
sentences.s659-s170: The king made his countenance restless; he filled his mouth with sweet drink.
sentences.s659-s171: Because of past events his heart grew tight (was pained); he writhed and his face became full of a strange discoloration.
sentences.s659-s172: Why, then, did he do him such cruelty? After that, from whom would one see love and fidelity?
sentences.s659-s49: He said to him, "Go and tell the wise man that from him are the famed rank and that reputation."
sentences.s659-s50: Why did you seek lesser preeminence? It became a jewel and an abusive judgment.
sentences.s659-s51: The devotee heard and came running; he reached the beauty-spot, swift and weary in spirit.
sentences.s659-s52: He sat before the king and spoke with him; thus he obtained from him an answer kept within.
sentences.s659-s53: That my condition is far greater than the condition of the king of the world, both openly and in secret.
sentences.s659-s54: The devotee turned back and took the answer, and he recounted the words one by one.
sentences.s659-s55: The king was greatly angered by the answers; he had him bound and (thrown) into a dark pit.
sentences.s659-s56: Again he asked that attendant how that petty-minded fate (the times) was behaving.
